octodns/record/caa.py.short_description: Defines CAA record value parsing, validation, and template support
octodns/record/caa.py.description: Implements CAA rdata handling via CaaValue: parses RFC6844-style "flags tag value" text, validates field ranges/presence, and supports parameter templating in the value. CaaRecord wires this value type into the generic Record/ValuesMixin framework and registers it for type dispatch.

key_constructs.purpose: Parses CAA rdata text into flags, tag, and value (supports whitespace and unquoting)

key_constructs.name: CaaValue.validate
key_constructs.purpose: Validates flags range and required fields for each CAA value object

key_constructs.name: CaaValue.template
key_constructs.purpose: Applies context templating to the CAA value when it contains '{...}'

key_constructs.name: CaaRecord
key_constructs.purpose: Record definition for DNS type CAA using CaaValue as its _value_type

key_constructs.name: Record.register_type(CaaRecord)
key_constructs.purpose: Registers the record type for parsing/dispatch by _type

key_behaviors: parses CAA rdata text
validates CAA fields
templates CAA values using parameters

insights.title: Add/verify template support for CA certificate value fields
insights.problem: CA records’ `CaaValue` needed consistent templating behavior for fields containing placeholders.
insights.root_cause: Template support either was missing or inconsistent for this value type.
insights.solution: Added tests verifying identity noop when no `{` exists and formatted output when placeholders exist (`ca.{needle}.net` → `ca.42.net`). (The diff indicates source changes occurred in record value implementations as part of the broader templating work.)
insights.lesson_learned: For templating across many record types, include tests that validate both identity semantics and correct formatting for the specific templated field(s).

insights.title: Fix CAA parsing to support values containing tags/whitespace
insights.problem: CAA RDATA parsing rejected/incorrectly split values when the value portion contained additional tokens (e.g., semicolon-separated tag-like content).
insights.root_cause: Parser used `split(' ')` into exactly 3 parts, so any additional whitespace in the value caused parsing failures or truncation.
insights.solution: Changed parsing from `flags, tag, value = value.split(' ')` to `flags, tag, value = value.split(' ', 2)` so the value keeps the remaining whitespace content.
insights.lesson_learned: When parsing DNS RDATA fields where the tail field may contain whitespace, split with a maxsplit limit to preserve the tail.

octodns/record/change.py.short_description: Represents create/update/delete changes between DNS record states
octodns/record/change.py.description: Defines Change and its subclasses Create, Update, and Delete to model the delta between an existing record and a desired record. Each subclass provides structured data for reporting/planning and a stable equality/comparison ordering via EqualityTupleMixin.

callers.context: next(c.record.name for c in plan.changes if isinstance(c, Update)),
next(c.record.name for c in plan.changes if isinstance(c, Create)),
next(c.record.name for c in plan.changes if isinstance(c, Delete)),

key_constructs.purpose: Produces a dict describing a create action for sync/plan output

key_constructs.name: Update.data

key_constructs.name: Delete.data

formats change summaries for sync plans

insights.title: Add Change.data for structured plan serialization
insights.problem: Checksum matching required a canonical representation of each change’s before/after data.
insights.root_cause: Change objects lacked a stable dict representation including type and embedded record data.
insights.solution: Added data properties to Create/Update/Delete: type + relevant record data keys (new/existing).
insights.lesson_learned: For plan hashing and API outputs, prefer explicit dict schemas (type + structured embedded payloads) over implicit object graphs.

It ensures consistent textual representation by round-tripping through the address type and supports record-field templating passthrough.

insights.title: Standardize template() noop behavior for IP address value types
insights.problem: Template method unnecessarily checked for `{` and could format values incorrectly; behavior needed to match other value types’ contracts.
insights.root_cause: For IP values, templating should be a noop; code previously had an always-no-placeholder safeguard that was redundant or incorrect vs the intended template contract.
insights.solution: Simplified `_IpValue.template` to always return `self` without attempting formatting.
insights.lesson_learned: Some value classes should never interpret templates; implement template as an intentional noop and lock it in with identity-based tests.

insights.title: Correct placeholder detection in SshfpValue.template
insights.problem: Placeholder detection logic was inverted (formatting returned early when placeholders existed).
insights.root_cause: Wrong `{`-presence condition in `SshfpValue.template`.
insights.solution: Switched the condition to return early only when `{` is absent; otherwise format and return new value.
insights.lesson_learned: For template conditional checks, standardize on `if '{' not in field: return self` across value types and enforce with tests.

insights.title: Fix template conditional for TLSA certificate association data
insights.problem: Template conditional was inverted, preventing formatting when placeholders were present.
insights.root_cause: In `TlsaValue.template`, return/format decision used the opposite `{` presence condition for templating.
insights.solution: Updated condition so templating is a noop when `{` is not present; otherwise, format and return a new value instance.
insights.lesson_learned: Use consistent templating semantics (noop when no placeholders) and enforce with tests that assert object identity vs replacement.

insights.title: Add/verify template support for DS digest values
insights.problem: DS digests may include placeholders that must be expanded via Value.template.

insights.lesson_learned: Digest-like values often appear in multiple record types; ensure templating logic is consistently applied and tested for each field.

insights.title: Add OPENPGPKEY record support (RFC 7929) with whitespace-tolerant parsing
insights.problem: Repository lacked a record implementation for OPENPGPKEY, and base64 key material in zone files may include whitespace/newlines.
insights.root_cause: No OPENPGPKEY record type existed; parsing didn’t exist to strip whitespace embedded in base64 fields.
insights.solution: Implemented `OpenpgpkeyValue.parse_rdata_text()` that strips spaces (base64 may be split across lines) and added `OpenpgpkeyRecord`/`OpenpgpkeyValue` with template support. Added tests for record creation, value parsing, and missing-value validation.
insights.lesson_learned: For records carrying encoded payloads (e.g., base64), account for zone-file formatting (whitespace/newlines) at parsing time and normalize early so equality/changes behave correctly.

insights.title: New URI record type with custom parsing/validation semantics
insights.problem: Adding a new record type affects how Record parsing/validation and templating/values work (new data model + new parsing errors).
insights.root_cause: Introduction of `UriValue`/`UriRecord` requires consistent integration with the generic Record framework, plus correct serialization (`rdata_text`) and template support.
insights.solution: Implemented `UriValue` parsing (`parse_rdata_text` expects `priority weight "target"`), validation of presence and numeric conversion, IDNA encoding of the target, templating support via `.template(params)`, and registered the record type via `Record.register_type(UriRecord)`. Added dedicated unit tests.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding record types, cover: parsing formats, validation errors, equality/hash behavior, and templating interactions; ensure registration hooks are correct.

insights.title: Verify templating for URLFWD path and target
insights.problem: URLFWD `path` and `target` should support placeholder expansion.
insights.root_cause: Templating contract needed coverage for fields that contain templatable patterns.
insights.solution: Added tests ensuring noop identity when no placeholders exist, and correct formatting for both `path` (`/{needle}`) and `target` (`http://foo.{needle}`).
insights.lesson_learned: Test each templated field within a complex record value; don’t rely on a single placeholder test for the entire object.

insights.title: Implement URLFWD UrlfwdValue.parse_rdata_text and rdata_text for canonical matching
insights.problem: URLFWD value parsing/serialization didn’t expose canonical rdata text in a uniform way, making it harder to implement generic RDATA-method expectations and filters/matchers.
insights.root_cause: URLFWD value lacked a parse_rdata_text/rdata_text pair consistent with other record value types.
insights.solution: Added UrlfwdValue.parse_rdata_text to split and unquote path/target and optionally parse code/masking/query as ints; added UrlfwdValue.rdata_text property to render the canonical text format.
insights.lesson_learned: Ensure every record value type implements the common parsing/serialization contract; tests should enforce that presence across all registered types.

insights.title: Add --checksum CLI argument for integrity-gated apply
insights.problem: Users needed an optional mechanism to ensure the plan being applied matches the expected computed plan.
insights.root_cause: Existing sync/apply flow had no built-in integrity binding across separate planning and applying steps.
insights.solution: Added --checksum argument to pass expected checksum to Manager.sync; improved help text to clarify interaction with --do/--dry-run.
insights.lesson_learned: Expose integrity features at the CLI level with clear help semantics so operators understand gating behavior.

insights.title: Rename SecretException to SecretsException
insights.problem: Base exception type naming didn’t match plural convention of the secret-handler subsystem.
insights.root_cause: Early naming mismatch between secret handler abstraction and its exception base class.
insights.solution: Renamed class SecretException -> SecretsException.
insights.lesson_learned: Base exception types form part of the external surface area; coordinate renames with all imports/tests in the batch.

tests/config-semis/escaped.semis.yaml.short_description: Fixture YAML testing escaped semicolons in TXT values
tests/config-semis/escaped.semis.yaml.description: Provides a YAML configuration where TXT record values include escaped semicolons (\;). It exists to verify the config parser preserves literal semicolons when escaping is used.

tests/config/zone-threshold.yaml.short_description: Zone config fixture defining update/delete percentage thresholds
tests/config/zone-threshold.yaml.description: Defines provider settings plus per-zone thresholds for when updates vs deletes should occur based on percentage change. It exists to exercise planning logic around update_pcent_threshold and delete_pcent_threshold across nested and default zones.

README.md.short_description: Project overview and documentation entry point for octoDNS
README.md.description: Explains octoDNS’s purpose (DNS as code across multiple providers) and points readers to the documentation and contribution guidelines. It exists as the primary landing page for users and contributors to understand the tool at a high level.

requirements.txt.description: Lists exact versions of runtime and tooling dependencies used by the project, with some environment markers for specific Python versions. It exists to ensure reproducible installs for development, testing, and documentation builds.

tests/config/include/dict_too.yaml.short_description: Fixture YAML used to validate !include loading order and parent merge
tests/config/include/dict_too.yaml.description: Contains a small dictionary with intentionally out-of-order keys to ensure included YAML files are loaded using the same loader/class as their parent. It exists to verify that custom YAML tags like !include behave consistently across nested structures.

tests/config/include/main.yaml.description: Uses custom YAML tags (!include) to pull in multiple other YAML files (array, dict, empty, nested, and subdirectory content). It exists to validate that include resolution and merging produce the expected final configuration.

tests/config-semis/unescaped.semis.yaml.short_description: Fixture YAML testing unescaped semicolons in TXT values
tests/config-semis/unescaped.semis.yaml.description: Provides a YAML configuration where TXT record values include semicolons without escaping. It exists to contrast with escaped.semis.yaml and ensure the parser/formatter handles or rejects unescaped semicolons according to expected behavior.

tests/config/dump-processors.yaml.short_description: Fixture config using processors (allowlist and unknown processor) during dump
tests/config/dump-processors.yaml.description: Configures YAML providers for input and dump targets and attaches record processors per zone. One zone uses a TypeAllowlistFilter while another references an unknown processor to test error handling and processor registration behavior.

tests/config/dynamic-config.yaml.short_description: Fixture config exercising dynamic/empty config fields and processor keys
tests/config/dynamic-config.yaml.description: Tests that sections with potentially None values or empty mappings (e.g., processors/post_processors/plan_outputs/secret_handlers) do not break config loading. tests/config/secrets.yaml.short_description: Fixture config for secret handlers and handler ordering constraints
tests/config/secrets.yaml.description: Defines dummy secret handlers with different prefixes, including cases that require environment variables versus other handlers (intentionally unsafe due to indeterminate ordering). It exists to verify how octoDNS wires secret_handlers and enforces/handles dependencies.

tests/config/alias-zone-loop.yaml.short_description: Fixture config testing alias chains and alias loops in zones

octodns/provider/yaml.py.short_description: Loads/writes DNS records from YAML files, optionally split per zone/record.
octodns/provider/yaml.py.description:
This module implements `YamlProvider`, a core provider that maps on-disk YAML configuration into `Zone` records and can apply planned changes back to YAML files. It is designed to integrate with the octoDNS provider/planning/apply pipeline: `populate()` loads data into a `Zone` (used as a source), and `_apply()` writes the post-change record state for the provider (used as a target).

The provider supports multiple record types dynamically via `Record.registered_types()` (so it does not hardcode YAML schema by type). It also supports special provider behaviors controlled by constructor parameters: enforcing deterministic YAML parse order (`enforce_order`, `order_mode`), inserting default TTLs (`default_ttl`), optionally handling semicolon escaping differences for TXT/SPF values (`escaped_semicolons`), and supporting both “single zone file” and “split zone files” layouts via `split_extension`. When `split_extension` is enabled, records are written into separate YAML files inside a subdirectory derived from the zone name, with special handling for “catchall” records (`CATCHALL_RECORD_NAMES`).

Notable integration details: `populate()` builds a deterministic list of source YAML files (split sources, zonefile, and optional `shared_filename`) and then calls `_populate_from_file()` for each. Record creation uses `Record.new(zone, name, d, source=self, lenient=lenient)` and records are inserted with `zone.add_record(... replace=self.populate_should_replace)`, which is how multi-provider override semantics are modeled in configuration. `_apply()` does not reimplement record reconciliation; it copies `plan.existing`, calls `copy.apply(plan.changes)`, then serializes `copy.records` sorted alphabetically. During serialization it strips `ttl` when it equals the provider’s `default_ttl`, and performs inverse semicolon unescaping when `escaped_semicolons` is disabled. The module also includes `SplitYamlProvider` as a deprecated compatibility wrapper (truncated in the provided snippet), which matters for external users/configs expecting the old class name/behavior.

key_constructs.name: YamlProvider
key_constructs.purpose: Provider implementation for populating and applying DNS record configuration via YAML files.
key_constructs.reasoning: It encapsulates file discovery, YAML parsing normalization, record instantiation, and serialization/write-back that must align with octoDNS planning/apply semantics and be deterministic for idempotency.

key_constructs.name: _apply
key_constructs.purpose: Apply a plan by mutating a copy of current zone state and writing resulting records to YAML on disk.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures provider output matches octoDNS zone mutation logic (`copy.apply(changes)`) and produces deterministic YAML content (sorted records, stripped default TTL, optional semicolon unescaping).

key_constructs.name: populate
key_constructs.purpose: Load records for a zone from one or more YAML files on disk (or skip if configured).
key_constructs.reasoning: Determines which YAML sources are used (split/zonefile/shared) and returns whether any sources existed; this return value and exception behavior are exercised by tests for optional vs required zone files.

key_constructs.name: _populate_from_file
key_constructs.purpose: Parse a single YAML file and add records to the provided `Zone`.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes YAML structure normalization (allowing singular vs list records), default TTL assignment, and TXT/SPF semicolon escaping adjustments before calling `Record.new()`.

key_constructs.name: _split_sources
key_constructs.purpose: Yield filenames for split-style zone record YAML files under the split directory.
key_constructs.reasoning: Implements UTF-8 vs IDNA directory selection and deterministically discovers `.yaml` record files inside the appropriate directory.

key_constructs.name: _zone_sources
key_constructs.purpose: Return the filename for a non-split zone YAML file (UTF-8 preferred unless missing).
key_constructs.reasoning: Implements UTF-8 vs IDNA file selection and detects an error case when both versions exist.

key_constructs.name: SplitYamlProvider
key_constructs.purpose: Deprecated provider class kept for backwards compatibility with older split configuration behavior.
key_constructs.reasoning: Config files or imports may reference this class; behavior should remain consistent with the documented deprecation path.

key_behaviors: Discovers zone YAML files based on `split_extension` and `disable_zonefile`
Parses YAML into record data and instantiates `Record` objects via `Record.new()`
Applies provider-level default TTL and optional semicolon escaping normalization
Supports multi-source overrides using `populate_should_replace` and `zone.add_record(replace=...)`
Serializes post-apply zone state into YAML with deterministic ordering and default TTL omission
Writes split-style record files and a catchall file when enabled

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the meaning of `populate()` return value or exception behavior for missing YAMLs without updating tests/config expectations.
pitfalls.consequence: Breaks `ignore_missing_zones` semantics and idempotency/plan expectations in tests (and potentially runtime behavior in multi-source pipelines).
pitfalls.prevention: When editing logic around source discovery and the `no YAMLs found` condition, ensure it stays aligned with `ignore_missing_zones` and how `target` usage is interpreted by the test suite.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying TXT/SPF semicolon escaping/unescaping behavior without considering both parsing and serialization branches of `escaped_semicolons`.
pitfalls.consequence: Round-trip YAML->records->YAML may not match fixtures; records could contain wrong value strings or trigger validation failures in record parsing.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat parsing (`_populate_from_file`) and apply (`_apply`) as paired transformations and verify both modes with the existing unit tests.

pitfalls.mistake: Allowing both UTF-8 and IDNA zone files/directories to exist simultaneously without raising.
pitfalls.consequence: Ambiguous discovery may load incorrect data or silently choose the wrong source; tests explicitly assert `ProviderException` in this condition.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the mutual-exclusion checks in `_split_sources()` and `_zone_sources()`.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering output file naming for split mode or catchall records (including the special `$[zone].yaml` filename behavior).
pitfalls.consequence: Breaks split-layout tests and can cause drift where repeated apply produces different file sets.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep filename derivation consistent with `split_extension`, `split_catchall`, and `CATCHALL_RECORD_NAMES`.

key_sections: YamlProvider.populate: source discovery and missing-zone handling
YamlProvider._populate_from_file: YAML parsing normalization (list vs singleton, ttl defaults, semicolon escaping)
YamlProvider._apply: plan mutation via Zone.copy/apply and deterministic YAML serialization + split write-out
skip_unless_needed: SplitYamlProvider: only relevant for deprecated class compatibility

relevant_snippets.description: Exercises populate/apply round-trip and idempotent plan behavior for YamlProvider.
Exercises IDNA vs UTF-8 filename selection and mutual exclusion errors.
Exercises ignore_missing_zones behavior and multi-provider source stacking.
Exercises enforce_order behavior by expecting YAML constructor errors when unsorted is enforced.
Exercises list_zones discovery for both split and non-split directories.

related_files.relationship: Validates populate/apply semantics, idna/utf8 mutual exclusion, ignore_missing_zones, and split directory discovery/write-out.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any behavioral edits in YamlProvider (source discovery, parsing/escaping, serialization/determinism, split filename conventions) need to preserve the expectations encoded in this test file.

insights.title: Add ignore_missing_zones to allow sources to skip missing YAML zone files
insights.problem: Using YamlProvider in a pipeline where some source directories may not contain every zone could fail the whole run when YAMLs are missing (even when that’s acceptable for optional sources).
insights.root_cause: YamlProvider.populate raised `ProviderException` if no YAMLs were found for the zone when neither `sources` nor `target` was provided.
insights.solution: Added `ignore_missing_zones` parameter (default False). When True and no YAMLs are found, `populate()` returns `False` and leaves the zone empty instead of raising.
insights.lesson_learned: For multi-source systems, model “optional sources” explicitly via configuration and add tests that cover both default behavior (raise) and opt-in behavior (skip/return False).

insights.title: TXT/SPF semicolon escaping behavior is incorrect when escaped_semicolons=false
insights.problem: When `escaped_semicolons` is disabled, TXT/SPF values containing semicolons should be accepted but the code still transformed the wrong key (`d['']`), leading to incorrect data and/or crashes depending on input shape.
insights.root_cause: In `YamlProvider.populate`, the transformation for semicolons used `d[''].replace(...)` instead of `d['value']` (and similarly for `values`), and tests were updated to reflect the intended behavior.
insights.solution: Fixed the key usage to operate on `d['value']` and `d['values']` only when `escaped_semicolons` is false, and added/updated YAML fixtures and tests to validate both modes.
insights.lesson_learned: Configuration flags that alter parsing/normalization paths are easy to break with small typos; ensure tests cover both branches of the flag and validate final record values rather than just parsing success.

insights.title: Detect double-escaped semicolons in chunked/TXT values
insights.problem: Double-escaped semicolons (`\\;`) can slip through and be interpreted incorrectly by downstream consumers.
insights.root_cause: Validation previously only checked for unescaped semicolons, not the specific case where backslashes are themselves escaped.
insights.solution: Added validation in `_ChunkedValue.validate` (TXT/chunked path) to detect `\\;`, then added fixtures and updated provider YAML test expectations to ensure a `ValidationError` is raised with the correct reason string.
insights.lesson_learned: When validating escaping rules, test both the single-escaped and double-escaped variants; otherwise, you may accept malformed input that looks superficially valid.

insights.title: Rework YamlProvider.populate return value based on whether sources were found/used
insights.problem: Inconsistent populate semantics: when used as a target, YamlProvider previously ignored existing YAML (forcing an always-false return) and also raised `no YAMLs found` even when target behavior should differ.
insights.root_cause: Populate logic conflated 'acting as target' with whether any YAML files should be considered 'existing'.
insights.solution: Removed unconditional early return when `target` is set; changed 'no YAMLs found' condition to only raise when `not sources and not target`. Updated logging and return value to reflect whether any YAML sources were found (`exists = len(sources) > 0`).
insights.lesson_learned: Be explicit about the meaning of return values like `populate_should_replace/exists`. Tests should encode the intended semantics for both source-mode and target-mode.

insights.title: Rework YamlProvider.apply to apply Zone.apply(changes) onto a copy
insights.problem: YamlProvider._apply previously constructed records directly from change objects, which could miss subtle interactions/ordering or produce inconsistent output compared to how the canonical Zone applies changes.
insights.root_cause: Re-implementing zone mutation logic inside the provider risked divergence from the canonical `Zone` behavior.
insights.solution: YamlProvider._apply now copies `plan.existing`, calls `copy.apply(changes)`, then writes out sorted `copy.records`. Also fixed downstream uses of desired.decoded_name to use `copy.decoded_name`.
insights.lesson_learned: Avoid re-implementing core state transition logic in providers; centralize mutations (here via `Zone.apply`) and reuse it consistently.

octodns/zone.py.short_description: Core Zone domain object for DNS records: validation, diffing, and applying changes.
octodns/zone.py.description:
This module defines the `Zone` class and related exceptions used throughout octoDNS to model the set of DNS records belonging to a single DNS zone. A Zone stores records grouped by “node” (record name) and enforces DNS correctness/constraints when records are added, including sub-zone ownership rules and CNAME coexistence rules. It also handles IDNA normalization internally, keeping both `name` (IDNA-encoded) and `decoded_name` for log/error messaging.

Zones integrate with the larger system by serving as the input/output domain object for providers/processors/managers. The `changes(desired, target)` method computes a list of `Create`, `Update`, and `Delete` change objects to transform one zone’s current records into another zone’s desired records, while respecting per-record include/exclude filtering and skipping records ignored by config. `Zone.apply(changes)` (invoked by tests and used elsewhere in the codebase) centralizes mutation semantics on Zone copies to ensure consistent application of create/update/delete operations.

This file also implements copy-on-write semantics: `Zone.copy()` creates a shallow copy tied to an origin zone, and the first mutating operation hydrates it. The `records` and `root_ns` properties are origin-aware. Because this semantics impacts when validations/state updates happen (e.g., root NS caching), edits to add/remove/changes logic must respect hydration behavior and any invariants those cached fields depend on.

key_constructs.name: SubzoneRecordException
key_constructs.purpose: Raised when a record that belongs to a configured sub-zone is added to the parent zone (except NS/DS at boundary).
key_constructs.reasoning: Callers and tests rely on this exception to enforce/diagnose ownership boundary violations. Its message includes `record.context` when present.

key_constructs.name: DuplicateRecordException
key_constructs.purpose: Raised when adding a record that duplicates an existing record by (name, type).
key_constructs.reasoning: It stores both `existing` and `new` record objects and formats the message with `context` for debugging; tests assert both exception fields and string formatting.

key_constructs.name: InvalidNodeException
key_constructs.purpose: Raised when CNAME records are added alongside other record types at the same node.
key_constructs.reasoning: The Zone.add_record logic uses this to enforce DNS standards while also supporting lenient behavior (warning vs raising).

key_constructs.name: InvalidNameError
key_constructs.purpose: Raised when zone names violate required formatting rules.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures early validation for zone naming (trailing dot, no double dots, no whitespace) before IDNA encoding/regex matching is used.

key_constructs.name: Zone
key_constructs.purpose: Container and operator for DNS records in a zone: IDNA normalization, sub-zone/CNAME validations, diffing, and applying changes.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the central domain object used by processors (templating), managers/planners, and provider apply flows. Its validations and copy-on-write behavior heavily influence correctness and tests.

key_constructs.name: hostname_from_fqdn
key_constructs.purpose: Extracts the hostname portion from an FQDN relative to the zone, handling both IDNA-encoded and UTF-8 inputs.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by `owns()` to determine sub-zone ownership; tests cover tricky IDNA boundary cases and whether the input’s encoding should determine the output’s encoding.

key_constructs.name: owns
key_constructs.purpose: Determines whether this zone should manage a given FQDN for a record type, respecting sub-zone boundaries.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encodes boundary rules: exact sub-zone name is owned only for NS (and elsewhere logic disallows non-NS/DS except at boundary). Tests validate several edge cases.

key_constructs.name: add_record
key_constructs.purpose: Adds a record to the zone with validation for sub-zone ownership and CNAME node coexistence; supports replace and lenient semantics.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is where exceptions are raised/warnings logged. It also updates internal cached state like `_root_ns` for apex NS.

key_constructs.name: remove_record
key_constructs.purpose: Removes a record from the zone; hydrates copy-on-write variants; clears `_root_ns` when removing apex NS.
key_constructs.reasoning: Mutation ordering and hydration semantics matter so that `records`/`root_ns` are consistent after apply/remove sequences.

key_constructs.name: changes
key_constructs.purpose: Computes required `Create`/`Update`/`Delete` operations to transform this zone into the desired zone, filtered by record ignore/include/exclude and provider support.
key_constructs.reasoning: The diff logic assumes Record hashing/comparison semantics and interacts with `target` provider support checks; tests depend on exact counts and change types.

key_behaviors: validates zone name formatting on construction (dot/double-dot/whitespace)
stores zone name internally as IDNA-encoded and keeps decoded name for messaging
normalizes FQDN/hostname handling in hostname_from_fqdn for both UTF-8 and IDNA
enforces sub-zone ownership rules in add_record (NS/DS allowed at boundary; other types rejected unless lenient)
enforces CNAME coexistence rules per node in add_record (warn or raise depending on lenient semantics)
tracks root NS record in `_root_ns` for empty hostname apex NS
supports copy-on-write via `_origin` hydration on first mutation
computes transforms between zones in changes() with include/exclude/ignored and provider support filtering
mutates zones via remove_record (and via apply elsewhere in codebase)

pitfalls.mistake: Treating zone.name as UTF-8/decoded text instead of IDNA-encoded internal representation.
pitfalls.consequence: Regex matching in hostname_from_fqdn/owns and record name storage can misbehave, causing incorrect ownership/diffing and breaking tests around IDNA.
pitfalls.prevention: When editing, preserve the contract that `self.name` is IDNA encoded and `self._records` are stored by `record.name` (already IDNA encoded).

pitfalls.mistake: Changing lenient behavior without accounting for both zone-level `lenient` argument and per-record `record.lenient`.
pitfalls.consequence: Violations that should warn vs raise may flip, breaking edge-case tests and downstream workflows that rely on lenient loading.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure validation gates combine `lenient` and `record.lenient` consistently in all boundary checks (sub-zone and CNAME node rules).

pitfalls.mistake: Breaking hydration/copy-on-write semantics by mutating internal structures before `hydrate()` or by bypassing origin-aware properties.
pitfalls.consequence: Copies may incorrectly share/mutate origin state, causing diff/apply tests to fail and producing inconsistent plan results.
pitfalls.prevention: Any change to record mutation must respect the early `if self._origin: self.hydrate()` checks used in add_record/remove_record.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering message formatting/context inclusion in DuplicateRecordException/SubzoneRecordException.
pitfalls.consequence: Tests assert exact string fragments (including `context` and `[UNKNOWN]` placeholders) and exception fields like `.existing/.new`.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve exception message construction logic and fields accessed by tests.

key_sections: Zone.__init__: validates input name, sets IDNA/regexes, initializes record storage and root NS cache
hostname_from_fqdn: IDNA vs UTF-8 path selection using ascii encode and regex stripping
owns: sub-zone boundary rules for NS/other types
add_record: hydration, sub-zone/type validation, duplicate detection, CNAME coexistence enforcement, root NS caching
changes: desired record hashing, include/exclude/ignored/provider-support filtering, diff computation
skip_unless_needed: Exception class docstrings (useful for understanding message expectations but not core runtime logic)
Deprecated `_remove_record` (tests cover current remove_record behavior)

relevant_snippets.description: Construction behavior: lowercasing/IDNA encoding and InvalidNameError cases.
hostname_from_fqdn behavior for missing trailing dot, UTF-8, and IDNA inputs.
add_record duplicate detection, replace=True semantics, and DuplicateRecordException message/context.
duplicate context handling across existing/new record context combinations.
changes() diff computation counts and update vs create/delete behavior; apply() via copy is validated.
owns() sub-zone boundary ownership edge cases and sub-zone record addition validations.
Sub-zone exception/warning semantics with lenient mode enabled at zone-level and record-level.
ignored records filtering in changes().
Templating processor uses Zone/Record interactions; ensure Zone mutations/validations remain compatible.

related_files.relationship: Direct unit tests validating Zone name/IDNA, owns(), add_record validations, and changes()/apply correctness.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any change to validation logic, exception message formatting, or diff/apply semantics will break exact assertions.

related_files.relationship: Uses Zone and Record to ensure templating works with CNAME and template variables.
related_files.reason_to_check: If you alter Zone record add/validation semantics (especially lenient handling), templating tests can be affected.

insights.title: Apply record-level lenience to sub-zone ownership exceptions
insights.problem: When a record inside a managed sub-zone violated ownership/type expectations, only zone-level `lenient` was honored; record-level `octodns.lenient` wasn’t applied, causing unexpected exceptions or missing warnings.
insights.root_cause: The check for sub-zone violations used `if lenient:` and didn’t incorporate `record.lenient` at that decision point.
insights.solution: Capture `new_lenient = record.lenient` and change conditions to `if lenient or new_lenient:` for sub-zone record/type exceptions/warnings.
insights.lesson_learned: If leniency can be configured at multiple layers (zone-level and record-level), always combine them consistently at all boundary checks, especially in ownership/sub-zone logic.

insights.title: CNAME coexistence now warns (not fails) when in lenient mode
insights.problem: Adding a CNAME alongside other records could raise `InvalidNodeException` even when lenient mode should allow relaxed/temporary invalid states.
insights.root_cause: The CNAME coexistence branch was unconditional raising when invalid state detected, without clearly gating on lenient combination conditions.
insights.solution: Adjusted the logic to compute `msg` and raise only when not lenient (and when existing/new records aren’t lenient), otherwise log `WARNING` and continue.
insights.lesson_learned: When lenient mode is meant to “allow and warn,” ensure all invalid state branches follow the same gating rules and log consistently rather than sometimes throwing and sometimes warning.

insights.title: Sub-zone records now warn instead of raising when in lenient mode
insights.problem: Records under managed sub-zones could raise `SubzoneRecordException` even when lenient mode was enabled, breaking the intended “warn and proceed” semantics.
insights.root_cause: The sub-zone exception handling raised regardless of lenient flag at the time; the commit introduced gating to switch between warning and exception.
insights.solution: Added conditional branches: when `lenient` is true, emit `self.log.warning(msg)`; otherwise raise `SubzoneRecordException`.
insights.lesson_learned: Lenient mode semantics must be consistent: if the project’s intent is ‘relaxed validation’ then exceptions should be downgraded to warnings wherever possible, not only for some record types.

insights.title: Allow CNAME coexistence when lenient=True for both existing and new records
insights.problem: Adding a CNAME alongside other record types at the same node could fail even when records were marked lenient, preventing legitimate “best-effort”/lenient workflows.
insights.root_cause: CNAME coexistence logic only allowed it when a non-lenient insertion met strict node rules, but did not correctly consider that all records being lenient should relax the CNAME restriction.
insights.solution: Adjusted `Zone.add_record` logic: computed `existing_lenient = all(r.lenient for r in node)` and changed the CNAME exclusion condition from `elif not lenient and (...)` to a condition that allows bypass when both existing and new are lenient (`and not (existing_lenient and new_lenient)`). Added tests covering: non-lenient A + non-lenient CNAME (still errors) and lenient/non-lenient combinations, including lenient+lenient success.
insights.lesson_learned: When introducing `lenient` semantics, ensure boundary-condition checks (like CNAME conflicts) consistently incorporate both existing and new record lenient flags.

insights.title: Add/remove pre-processing record snapshot for alias-zone processor compatibility
insights.problem: Alias zones interacting with processors needed access to raw/pre-processed records; this required additional Zone state.
insights.root_cause: Without a dedicated snapshot field, alias zone record copying could occur after other processors had mutated values, breaking processors like templating that rely on predictable input fields.
insights.solution: Added `Zone._pre_processing_records` to store a frozen copy set by Manager before processors run; later removed it in the revert batch.
insights.lesson_learned: If Zone/Manager interactions require special ordering semantics (pre- vs post-processor), encapsulate that state carefully—adding it can fix correctness but must be retained consistently or reverted with care.

insights.title: Introduce Zone.apply(changes) to centralize mutation logic
insights.problem: State mutation logic for applying plan changes was duplicated (at least in YamlProvider), creating risk of behavioral drift and harder-to-test correctness.
insights.root_cause: No shared method existed for applying `Create/Update/Delete` changes to a Zone; each caller implemented its own mutation strategy.
insights.solution: Added `Zone.apply(self, changes)` that iterates changes and applies deletes via `remove_record(change.existing)` and applies other changes via `add_record(change.new, replace=True, lenient=True)`. Tests validate that applying changes to a copy matches the target and leaves the original unchanged.
insights.lesson_learned: When multiple components need to apply the same mutation semantics, provide a single canonical helper on the domain object and test it directly.

insights.title: Zone-specific thresholds introduced (new Zone constructor parameters and copy semantics)
insights.problem: Adding zone-level thresholds changes how Zone instances are constructed/copied and how Plan safety thresholds are computed.
insights.root_cause: Existing architecture assumed provider-level thresholds only. New feature required storing thresholds on Zone and propagating them through Zone.copy.
insights.solution: Extended `Zone.__init__` with `update_pcent_threshold`/`delete_pcent_threshold` and ensured `Zone.copy()` preserves these fields. Manager populates Zone with config-derived values when zones are requested.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding new state to core domain objects (Zone), update all construction sites and copy-on-write paths; add tests for precedence and default/unset behavior.

octodns/processor/templating.py.short_description: Processor that applies Python .format-style templating to record values using context vars.
octodns/processor/templating.py.description:
This file defines a `Templating` processor used in the octoDNS processing pipeline to substitute template expressions in record values. Templates are evaluated by delegating to each value’s `.template(params)` method (not raw Python `.format` directly), allowing value types (e.g., TXT vs record types with multiple fields) to decide how templating is applied to their internal representation.

The processor exists to build a consistent parameter dictionary for formatting: it always includes zone-level identifiers (decoded/encoded forms), counts (like `zone_num_records`), and record-level identifiers (decoded/encoded FQDNs, name, type, ttl, and `record_source_id`). It also merges any user-provided `context` values from processor configuration, where callable context values are invoked with `(desired, provider)` at processing time so that dynamic context can be computed.

A key behavioral constraint is the `trailing_dots` flag. When disabled, the processor strips the final trailing dot from all built-in FQDN-like variables used in templates (zone name/encoded/decoded, and record fqdn variants). This impacts the exact resulting substituted strings and is validated in unit tests. The processor also handles template parameter errors by wrapping missing keys (`KeyError`) into a domain-specific `TemplatingError` that includes the record’s FQDN and the undefined parameter name.

Finally, when templating changes values, the processor updates the desired zone by copying the record with `lenient=lenient` and uses `desired.add_record(..., replace=True, lenient=lenient)` so downstream validation semantics align with the effective `lenient` value (processor-level `self.lenient` combined with the method argument). It explicitly skips templating for custom value types that do not expose a `.template` method on their value objects.

key_constructs.name: TemplatingError
key_constructs.purpose: Exception type raised when a template references an undefined parameter.
key_constructs.reasoning: The tests assert on the exact string formatting and include the record FQDN and the missing key name; any change must preserve this contract.

key_constructs.name: Templating
key_constructs.purpose: OctoDNS processor that templates record value strings using a computed params dict.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the processor entry point called by the processing pipeline; its parameter construction, trailing dot normalization, and lenient propagation are observable via tests.

key_constructs.name: process_source_and_target_zones
key_constructs.purpose: Iterates desired zone records and applies templating to value(s), updating the desired zone when substitutions occur.
key_constructs.reasoning: This method defines the complete behavioral surface: how params are built, how/when templating is skipped, how exceptions are raised, and how lenient mode is preserved during record updates.

key_behaviors: builds a formatting parameter dict from zone and record metadata
optionally strips trailing dots from FQDN-like built-in variables
invokes user-provided callable context values using (desired, provider)
templates record.value when record lacks a 'values' field
templates each item in record.values when record has a 'values' field
skips templating when value objects do not support .template
raises TemplatingError when a referenced template key is missing
updates the desired zone with replace semantics when templating changes values
propagates effective lenient mode into copied records and add_record

pitfalls.mistake: Changing how `trailing_dots` affects the built-in variable values without updating the associated contract
pitfalls.consequence: Templates will substitute different strings than expected, breaking user configurations and unit tests (notably `test_trailing_dots`).
pitfalls.prevention: Treat `trailing_dots` normalization as an API contract for `zone_name`/`record_*_fqdn`-style variables; ensure any modification preserves the exact expected string outputs.

pitfalls.mistake: Catching exceptions other than `KeyError` or altering how `TemplatingError` message is composed
pitfalls.consequence: The user-facing error message and the tests asserting exact wording will diverge, and debugging info will degrade.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the `TemplatingError` initialization logic that embeds `record.fqdn` and the missing key name from `e.args[0]`.

pitfalls.mistake: Failing to propagate lenient mode when copying or re-adding modified records
pitfalls.consequence: Record validation/lenience semantics may run under the wrong strictness level, potentially causing failures or different behavior during processing.
pitfalls.prevention: When templating changes values, keep the `lenient=lenient` propagation in both `record.copy(...)` and `desired.add_record(...)` paths.

pitfalls.mistake: Attempting to template custom value objects that don't implement `.template`
pitfalls.consequence: Processing can fail or produce incorrect results; current behavior intentionally skips such values.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the `.template` presence checks on value objects; only values with a `.template` method are processed.

key_sections: TemplatingError: exception formatting contract for missing template parameters
Templating.__init__: configuration inputs (trailing_dots, context) and stored state
process_source_and_target_zones: params construction, trailing dot handling, templating application loops, and desired zone updates
skip_unless_needed: import statements
long docstring examples (informational only)

relevant_snippets.description: Covers templating for CNAME/TXT, skipping values without .template, trailing dot parameter normalization, context callables, and exact TemplatingError messaging.

related_files.relationship: Direct unit tests that lock the processor’s behavior: template substitution, trailing dot trimming, context callable invocation, skip behavior for unsupported value types, and exact error messages.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any behavioral adjustment in params building, trailing dot normalization, or exception formatting should be validated against these assertions.

insights.title: Templating processor passes lenient into record copies/adds
insights.problem: Templating could trigger validations/warnings that should only occur after templating substitution, and/or could incorrectly enforce strict validation requiring record-level lenience.
insights.root_cause: When templating modified record values, the processor used `record.copy()` and then assigned `.value(s)` without passing the current lenience context, so the new record could be re-validated under strict rules.
insights.solution: Updated the templating processor to construct updated records via `record.copy(values=new_values, lenient=lenient)` / `record.copy(value=new_value, lenient=lenient)` and to call `desired.add_record(..., lenient=lenient)` where appropriate.
insights.lesson_learned: When processors transform records, preserve and propagate leniency/validation mode into both the copied record and `Zone.add_record` call sites; otherwise boundary validation may run too early or with the wrong strictness.

insights.title: templating processor introduces trailing_dots parameter with changed variable formatting
insights.problem: Trailing dot handling for built-in templating variables changed over time (rstrip vs leaving dots), and alias between variable variants could break template expectations.
insights.root_cause: Built-in variables (zone_name/record_fqdn etc.) were derived using `.rstrip('.')` and later refactored to slice the last character when `trailing_dots` is disabled, affecting the exact output values embedded in templates.
insights.solution: Added `trailing_dots` parameter to `Templating.__init__` (default True). When `trailing_dots` is False, built-in FQDN-like variables are trimmed by removing the final trailing dot via string slicing for both zone and record variables.
insights.lesson_learned: When changing string normalization of template variables (especially around trailing dots/FQDNs), treat it as an API contract: add explicit configuration and update tests to lock behavior for both modes.

insights.title: Unknown templating parameters now raise a record-specific error
insights.problem: When templates reference missing keys, users would likely see raw KeyError without context about which record/template value failed.
insights.root_cause: Template evaluation used `value.template(params)` (and underlying format calls) directly; missing keys propagated as KeyError without a structured, user-friendly message.
insights.solution: Added `TemplatingError(record, msg)` and wrapped template application to catch `KeyError` and raise `TemplatingError` with message `Invalid record "<fqdn>", undefined template parameter "<key>" in value`.
insights.lesson_learned: Template engines should surface errors with enough context (record + parameter name) to make debugging feasible; convert low-level exceptions into domain-specific ones.

insights.title: Refactor templating parameter building to avoid duplicating trailing_dots logic
insights.problem: Previous implementations had repeated conditional merges for zone/record params based on trailing_dots, making it easy to get inconsistent values or update only part of the variable set.
insights.root_cause: Conditional updates to `zone_params` and `record_params` were split across multiple branches and later changed again, leading to churn and potential inconsistencies.
insights.solution: In the later trailing_dots change, variable computation was refactored to compute base variables, then conditionally slice them once per zone/record before constructing the params dict.
insights.lesson_learned: When multiple outputs depend on the same normalization flag, compute the normalized base values once, then build the params dict from those normalized values to prevent partial fixes.

tests/test_octodns_processor_templating..short_description: Unit tests validating templating substitutions, trailing dot handling, context injection, and errors.
tests/test_octodns_processor_templating..description:
This test module verifies the `octodns.processor.templating.Templating` processor end-to-end at the record/zone level. It constructs `Zone` objects, creates records using `Record.new(...)`, adds them to the zone, then calls `Templating.process_source_and_target_zones(...)`. Assertions inspect the updated records in the returned desired zone to confirm that template variables were substituted correctly and that non-templatable value types are skipped.

The file includes two custom record/value types (`Single` and `Multiple`) that use `CustomValue` (subclass of `str`) to track which attributes are queried. The `CustomValue.__getattr__` records requested attribute names and raises `AttributeError`, which allows the `test_no_template` test to confirm that the templating processor checks for the presence of a `.template` method and does so on the expected value instances (both record-level `value` and per-item `values`).

The trailing dot behavior is locked using a patched `octodns.record.TxtValue.template` method. `test_trailing_dots` validates that when `trailing_dots=False`, the params passed into `.template(...)` contain trimmed FQDN-like variables (e.g., `zone_name` is `unit.tests` without a trailing dot, and `record_fqdn` is also trimmed). Context injection is tested in `test_context`, including callable context values receiving `(desired, provider)` and returning dynamic template substitutions. Finally, `test_bad_key` asserts exact `TemplatingError` string contents for both a record with `value` and a record with `lenient=True` to ensure errors are raised consistently across record types.

These tests collectively define the processor’s observable contract: correct params dictionary keys, correct trimming rules under `trailing_dots=False`, strict error messaging format for missing template parameters, and correct leniency propagation into record copies/add operations indirectly through behavior (e.g., using `lenient=True` record creation when template validation would otherwise be strict).

key_constructs.name: TemplatingTest
key_constructs.purpose: Test suite for the Templating processor.
key_constructs.reasoning: Defines all relevant assertions for the processor contract; editing the processor should keep these tests passing.

key_constructs.name: CustomValue
key_constructs.purpose: Custom value type used to test `.template` capability detection and templating skipping.
key_constructs.reasoning: Its `__getattr__` records requested attributes and intentionally fails, allowing the test to verify which objects the processor checks for templating support.

key_constructs.name: Single
key_constructs.purpose: Record type with a single `value` field backed by `CustomValue`.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used in `test_no_template` to ensure `.template` presence is checked on `s.value`.

key_constructs.name: Multiple
key_constructs.purpose: Record type with a `values` list field backed by `CustomValue`.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used in `test_no_template` to ensure `.template` presence is checked per element in `m.values`.

key_constructs.name: _find
key_constructs.purpose: Helper to locate a record by name within a zone’s records.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used widely to read back templated record outputs for assertions.

key_behaviors: validates template substitution for record types with `value`
validates template substitution for record types with `values`
verifies processor skips templating for unsupported custom value types
asserts exact params passed into TxtValue.template when trailing_dots is disabled
asserts context callables are invoked with (desired, provider)
asserts exact TemplatingError message formatting for undefined template parameters

pitfalls.mistake: Altering the parameter names or trimming logic for built-in template variables without updating the corresponding test assertions
pitfalls.consequence: `test_trailing_dots` will fail due to mismatch in the `call({...})` expected params structure.
pitfalls.prevention: Any processor change must preserve the contract of which keys exist in params and their exact values under both trailing_dots modes.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing error string formatting for missing template parameters
pitfalls.consequence: `test_bad_key` compares `str(ctx.exception)` to an exact string; even minor message differences will fail tests.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the `TemplatingError` message structure stable with record fqdn and missing key name.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying how templating support is detected for custom values
pitfalls.consequence: `test_no_template` relies on `CustomValue._asked_for` to equal `{'template'}`; different detection logic will break this assertion.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the behavior that results in the `.template` attribute being checked on record value(s) that do not implement it.

key_sections: CustomValue/Single/Multiple: fixtures for `.template` capability detection
test_trailing_dots: patched TxtValue.template and strict expected params dict
test_context: callable context injection and provider passing
test_bad_key: exact TemplatingError message assertions
skip_unless_needed: _find helper (simple lookup)
test_cname/test_txt (basic substitution checks)

relevant_snippets.description: test_no_template: asserts that value types were asked for the 'template' attribute.
test_trailing_dots: patching TxtValue.template and asserting exact params passed in.
test_context: callable context values and provider passing into the context callables.
test_bad_key: exact error message for undefined template parameter on both value and lenient record types.

related_files.relationship: Implements the behavior under test; tests lock on params structure, trimming, and error messaging.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any change to templating param construction or error handling should be validated against these assertions.

insights.title: Tests lock template parameter error messaging and trailing dot behavior
insights.problem: Template processing previously could raise raw KeyError or produce confusing errors; trailing dot normalization was prone to breaking changes.
insights.root_cause: Template evaluation depends on correct parameter dictionaries and consistent normalization; missing parameters caused KeyError without context.
insights.solution: Added tests: `test_bad_key` asserts `TemplatingError` message includes invalid record and missing parameter; added/updated `test_trailing_dots` to validate `{zone_name}` substitution under trailing_dots=False.
insights.lesson_learned: Make tests assert user-facing error strings for template engines so debugging quality doesn’t regress.

octodns/record/base.py.short_description: Core DNS Record factory/validation plus shared mixins for value(s) handling.
octodns/record/base.py.description:
This module defines the abstract `Record` base class used throughout octodns for representing DNS records (A, CNAME, ALIAS, dynamic records, etc). Its primary responsibilities are: registering record subclasses by DNS RR type, constructing concrete record instances via `Record.new()`, validating record metadata (name/FQDN/TTL/healthcheck), and converting multiple parsed RRs into a single normalized record via `from_rrs()`. It also centralizes IDNA normalization behavior by encoding record names internally with `idna_encode()` and keeping a decoded version for logging/errors.

Two mixins in the same module, `ValuesMixin` and `ValueMixin`, define how records with multiple values vs a single value are validated, initialized, serialized, and compared for change detection. These mixins rely on per-record-type `_value_type` objects (e.g., `ARecord` uses an IPv4 value type) to parse and validate individual rdata strings, and they normalize input shapes so the rest of the pipeline can operate on consistent internal `self.values` / `self.value` structures. Serialization is implemented in `_data()` and is sensitive to how the schema should represent 0/1/N values: for `ValuesMixin`, the output uses `value` when there’s exactly one surviving value and `values` otherwise, including empty-list behavior.

The module is also where octodns-specific metadata is handled: records carry an `octodns` dict (from input `data['octodns']`), with convenience properties like `ignored`, `included`, and healthcheck-derived helpers. Tests in `tests/test_octodns_record.py` and `tests/test_octodns_record_dynamic.py` exercise key behaviors: record type registration errors, IDNA UTF-8 handling, `Record.from_rrs` grouping, `Record.copy` deep-copy isolation and empty-value semantics, and healthcheck defaulting/overrides for HTTP/HTTPS vs ICMP/TCP/UDP.

key_constructs.name: unquote
key_constructs.purpose: Remove surrounding quotes from a string RDATA component.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by value parsers (and historically by record value parsing) to support backends that emit quoted rdata text; callers must ensure the argument is a string or that non-string values are handled safely.

key_constructs.name: Record
key_constructs.purpose: Abstract DNS record base: registration, validation, construction, and shared helpers.
key_constructs.reasoning: All concrete record types are registered into `Record._CLASSES` and instantiated via `Record.new`; changes to constructor/factory semantics affect parsing, providers, diffing, and tests that assume normalized idna names and consistent data serialization.

callers.context: self, record: Record, values: List[str], lookups: int = 0

key_constructs.name: Record.register_type
key_constructs.purpose: Register a concrete record subclass for a DNS RR type string.
key_constructs.reasoning: Prevents duplicate RR type registration; tests assert the error message includes the module/class that previously registered the type.

key_constructs.name: Record.registered_types
key_constructs.purpose: Expose the internal mapping of RR type strings to registered classes.
key_constructs.reasoning: Test code checks presence/absence of types to confirm registration behavior; altering the mapping shape or timing would break expectations.

key_constructs.name: Record.new
key_constructs.purpose: Factory that validates and instantiates the correct record subclass from raw config data.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes multiple concerns: IDNA encoding/decoding, whitespace checks, missing/unknown type errors, base validation plus subtype validation, lenient vs strict behavior, and passing through `source`/`context`.

key_constructs.name: Record.validate
key_constructs.purpose: Validate shared record-level constraints (name, fqdn length, labels length, ttl, healthcheck protocol).
key_constructs.reasoning: Produces a list of human-readable `reasons` that are either raised as `ValidationError` or logged when `lenient` is enabled; tests rely on precise reason content in other modules.

key_constructs.name: Record.from_rrs
key_constructs.purpose: Convert a sequence of parsed `Rr` objects into normalized `Record` objects, grouping by (name,type).
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures multi-rdata records (e.g., A with multiple IPs) are combined; it derives record name from `zone.hostname_from_fqdn` and uses subtype `data_from_rrs` to produce the `data` dict.

callers.context: (r._type, r.name): r for r in Record.from_rrs(zone, rrs, source=99)

key_constructs.name: ValuesMixin
key_constructs.purpose: Mixin for record types whose data contains multiple rdata values.
key_constructs.reasoning: Defines how to coerce `value`/`values` inputs into `self.values`, how to validate list contents, how to compare for changes, and how to serialize back to the expected schema keys (`value` vs `values`).

key_constructs.name: ValuesMixin.validate
key_constructs.purpose: Validate record and its list of values using `_value_type.validate`.
key_constructs.reasoning: Depends on consistent interpretation of `values` vs `value` fields; downstream behavior assumes `reasons` are merged with base validation reasons.

key_constructs.name: ValuesMixin.data_from_rrs
key_constructs.purpose: Create a standardized `data` dict from grouped RRs (ttl/type from first rr, values parsed from all rrs).
key_constructs.reasoning: Used by `Record.from_rrs` to normalize multi-rdata records; changing its output keys/shape affects `Record.new` instantiation.

key_constructs.name: ValuesMixin.__init__
key_constructs.purpose: Initialize `self.values` from `data['values']` or `data['value']`, then sort processed values.
key_constructs.reasoning: `self.values` order is normalized via sorting, affecting equality/change comparisons and deterministic serialization.

key_constructs.name: ValuesMixin._data
key_constructs.purpose: Serialize record into a dict with correct `value`/`values` key choice based on resulting list length.
key_constructs.reasoning: Implements an explicit schema invariant that influences provider round-trips and tests (e.g., empty inputs produce `values: []`, not missing keys).

key_constructs.name: ValueMixin
key_constructs.purpose: Mixin for record types whose data contains a single rdata value.
key_constructs.reasoning: Defines how to validate and initialize `self.value`, how to compare changes, and how to serialize with an explicit `value` key.

callers.context: from octodns.record import Record, ValueMixin, ValuesMixin

key_behaviors: Registers record subclasses by RR type and rejects duplicate registrations
Encodes record names using IDNA during construction
Validates fqdn/name/ttl and healthcheck protocol unless overridden via lenient mode
Constructs records using subclass validate plus subtype-specific validation
Combines multiple parsed RRs into one Record using grouping by (rr.name, rr._type)
Exposes `ignored`, `excluded`, and `included` from `octodns` metadata
Computes healthcheck host/path/protocol/port based on `octodns.healthcheck` and defaults
Detects changes by comparing `ttl` and (in mixins) `value(s)`

pitfalls.mistake: Changing truthiness checks around `Record.copy` inputs or value presence
pitfalls.consequence: Empty-but-explicit overrides (e.g., value '' or values []) can be treated as “not provided,” producing wrong record data and failing tests that cover empty values/copy semantics.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve `None` vs empty handling semantics; tests explicitly assert behavior for `values: []` and for scalar vs list normalization.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering the internal `idna_encode` normalization or removing `decoded_name` usage
pitfalls.consequence: Breaks UTF-8/IDNA behavior in tests and causes incorrect `fqdn`/error messages; providers may also produce mismatched names.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain the contract that `Record.name` is stored encoded and `decoded_name` is kept for logs/errors and `decoded_fqdn`.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing `ValuesMixin._data` key selection (`value` vs `values`) or empty-list serialization rules
pitfalls.consequence: Breaks provider YAML round-trip expectations and tests that assert keys exist (or don’t) for specific record types and empty-value boundaries.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the schema invariant that serialization is deterministic at 0/1/many; align with existing tests for `test_values_mixin_data` and YAML provider expectations.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing/altering octodns metadata deep-copy behavior through `_data()`/`copy()`
pitfalls.consequence: Mutations to the dict returned from `record.data` may leak back into the record’s internal state; tests verify deep-copy isolation and that edits to nested dicts change `data` as expected.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure `Record._data` includes `octodns` and uses `deepcopy` to prevent shared references.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying healthcheck helper defaults or protocol gating logic
pitfalls.consequence: Changes `healthcheck_path/host/port` behavior for protocols other than HTTP/HTTPS and affects multiple dynamic-record tests.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve default protocol = 'HTTPS' and port = 443, and protocol gating (host/path only meaningful for HTTP/HTTPS; port None for ICMP).

key_sections: Record.new: IDNA encoding, type lookup, lenient validation behavior, context propagation
Record.validate: shared constraints (name/fqdn/ttl/healthcheck protocol)
Record.from_rrs: grouping and record data normalization via subtype data_from_rrs
ValuesMixin and ValueMixin: input normalization, `changes()` behavior, and `_data()` serialization invariants
Healthcheck helpers: defaults and protocol-specific behavior
skip_unless_needed: Equality tuple/hash comments unless changing Record comparison/diffing behavior

relevant_snippets.description: Record type registration and missing/unknown type error handling via Record.new
IDNA/UTF-8 encoding expectations in Record.new and healthcheck-related fqdn/decoded_fqdn usage
Record.from_rrs grouping behavior and `ValuesMixin.data_from_rrs` correctness
ValuesMixin._data serialization rules for empty values vs presence/absence of value keys
Record.copy deep-copy isolation via Record.data (octodns dict deep-copy semantics)
Dynamic record copy preserving octodns metadata and healthcheck defaults/overrides

related_files.relationship: Directly tests Record registration, construction, serialization shape, and copy/deepcopy behaviors tied to this module.
related_files.reason_to_check: If editing any normalization/serialization/validation logic in base Record/Value mixins, these tests define the behavioral contract.

related_files.relationship: Uses Record.healthcheck_* helpers and validates dynamic record handling that depends on base Record.octodns semantics.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any changes to healthcheck defaults/protocol gating can cascade into these dynamic record tests.

related_files.relationship: Asserts provider round-trip schema invariants for `value` vs `values` keys which are produced by ValuesMixin/_data here.
related_files.reason_to_check: Serialization key selection differences will surface as YAML provider mismatches.

insights.title: Record.copy now supports empty/falsey value(s)
insights.problem: Record.copy previously used `if value:` / `elif values:` which treated empty strings/lists as “not provided,” preventing intended overrides and leading to incorrect record data when overriding with empty values.
insights.root_cause: Truthiness checks rather than explicit `is not None` checks.
insights.solution: Changed conditionals to `if values is not None:` / `elif value is not None:` so empty-but-explicit overrides are respected and the correct `value`/`values` field is set.
insights.lesson_learned: When API semantics distinguish “not provided” vs “provided but empty,” prefer explicit `is None` checks over truthiness checks.

insights.title: Record._octodns deprecated in favor of Record.octodns (backcompat via properties)
insights.problem: Consumers might rely on Record._octodns, but a new public attribute name Record.octodns was introduced.
insights.root_cause: API evolution toward a clearer, non-private attribute required migration off `_octodns` without immediately breaking callers.
insights.solution: Added Record.octodns as the canonical attribute and kept `_octodns` as a deprecated property wrapper (getter/setter) that forwards to `octodns`. Updated internal logic to use `octodns` everywhere.
insights.lesson_learned: When renaming fields, deprecate via properties rather than instant breaks; also update tests to validate both the new and backcompat paths.

insights.title: Normalize Record value vs values handling (singular/list/tuple; dict config variants)
insights.problem: Record values could be provided as a single string under `values`, causing accidental character-by-character expansion or inconsistent validation behavior.
insights.root_cause: Inconsistent handling of 'value' vs 'values' and type assumptions: some code validated expecting list/tuple but configuration commonly uses a single value (type-o).
insights.solution: Added validation/normalization: warn/fail when `values` is a string (earlier change), then later reworked to treat `value` and `values` interchangeably and coerce scalars into a list internally so downstream always sees a list. Special-cased cases like PTR/target by relying on updated value-type behavior.
insights.lesson_learned: For config ergonomics, explicitly normalize scalar-vs-list inputs at the boundary so the rest of the pipeline can be consistent and not type-sensitive.

insights.title: Record.copy/value parsing handled empty values list [] incorrectly
insights.problem: Record.copy and/or Record initialization could mis-handle records where 'values' was present but empty ([]) leading to missing expected fields or incorrect value list construction.
insights.root_cause: Values initialization logic attempted to treat 'value' vs 'values' inconsistently; when neither 'values' nor 'value' existed as expected in lenient paths, it raised KeyError instead of producing an empty list.
insights.solution: Adjusted ValuesMixin initialization parsing in octodns/record/base.py so that when 'values' isn’t present and 'value' also isn’t present, it defaults to values = [] (instead of erroring). Also ensured sorting/processing still occurs.
insights.lesson_learned: Treat empty collections (like values: []) as first-class valid inputs in both strict and lenient modes; ensure initialization paths converge on consistent internal representation (e.g., self.values == []).

insights.title: Record.data now includes octodns special section data; deep copy semantics corrected
insights.problem: Record.data omitted the `octodns` subsection, and Record.copy relied on .data, so non-public provider extensions using octodns metadata could behave incorrectly (especially in tests where data/copy are abused).
insights.root_cause: Record._data returned only ttl/context and did not include self._octodns; and Record.new/copy flow depended on Record.data for heavy lifting.
insights.solution: Updated Record._data to include deepcopy(self._octodns) under the 'octodns' key when present. Adjusted handling of context to wrap the combined dict. Removed redundant insertion of octodns in Record.new path.
insights.lesson_learned: If you maintain an internal metadata channel (like _octodns), ensure it is represented consistently in the public serialization (data) and deep-copied where copy/transform semantics depend on it.

insights.title: ValuesMixin._data serialization logic for 0/1/many values normalized
insights.problem: Record serialization in data did not handle the boundary between single-value and multi-value consistently; the representation could omit values when it should include them (or vice versa).
insights.root_cause: The previous implementation only set 'values' when len(self.values) > 1 and len(values) > 1; cases like empty list or None-filtering led to inconsistent output keys.
insights.solution:
Flipped logic:
- If len(self.values) == 1, set ret['value'] when that single entry is truthy
- Else (len != 1), compute filtered values from self.values, then:
  - if exactly one survives, set ret['value']
  - else set ret['values'] (including empty list case)
This makes behavior deterministic and testable.
insights.lesson_learned: When serializing optional/collection-like fields, explicitly define how empty and filtered cases should appear in the output schema (e.g., always include 'values': [] for empty inputs).

insights.title: Support unquoting parsed RDATA text (PowerDNS compatibility)
insights.problem: Providers (notably PowerDNS) may emit quoted RDATA components; parsing logic expected raw/unquoted values leading to mismatches or incorrect normalization.
insights.root_cause: RDATA value parsing did not consistently strip surrounding quotes from string fields for multiple record types.
insights.solution: Added unquote(s) helper in octodns/record/base.py and updated record value parsers (CaaValue, LocValue, MxValue, NaptrValue, SrvValue, SshfpValue, TlsaValue) to call unquote on relevant string components.
insights.lesson_learned: When interoperating with multiple DNS backends, account for provider-specific quoting/escaping differences; centralize unquote logic to keep parsing consistent.

insights.title: Record.from_rrs now accepts source parameter
insights.problem: Record construction from RRS lacked a way to carry the origin identifier/source through to the produced Record objects.
insights.root_cause: Record.from_rrs did not allow callers to attach metadata like source to created records.
insights.solution: Extended Record.from_rrs signature to include source=None and passed source to Record.new(...).
insights.lesson_learned: If the system uses Record.source for tracking provenance, ensure bulk-construction helpers (like from_rrs) can populate it too.

octodns/manager.py.description:
This module defines the central `Manager` class that loads an octoDNS YAML config, instantiates configured providers, sources/secret handlers/plan outputs, and then orchestrates zone sync operations end-to-end. It also manages global processors/post-processors, optional processors like `AutoArpa` and `MetaProcessor`, executor configuration (threaded vs main-thread), IDNA/UTF-8 zone handling, and optional safety gating via plan checksum enforcement.

The manager’s workflow is driven by helpers that resolve classes/kwargs from config (`_get_named_class`, `_import_module`, `_build_kwargs`), normalize/validate config structure (`_config_zones` using `IdnaDict`), and compute which configured sub-zones to include (`configured_sub_zones`). It also supports compatibility fallbacks and deprecations (via the imported `deprecated`) for older provider/source method signatures, plus specialized processors for templating/meta/arpa. The planning and applying phases rely on `Plan` and provider methods (`BaseProvider`), with support for eligibility filtering across zones/sources/targets and for alias zones.

`_AggregateTarget` is a small helper used to aggregate multiple provider “targets” and determine combined capability/support behavior (via intersection of SUPPORTS_* booleans and a strict `supports(record)` predicate requiring all targets to support a record). `MainThreadExecutor`/`MakeThreadFuture` provide a uniform future-based interface so that code can treat sync tasks “as if async” even when run on the main thread.

When editing this file, the most important constraints are: (1) config parsing/kwargs expansion (especially `env/<VAR>`-style secrets and nested dict recursion), (2) correct IDNA normalization and sub-zone enumeration ordering, (3) checksum serialization stability/verification behavior affecting apply safety, and (4) maintaining test-observable semantics in sync/dump/compare and eligibility filtering. The module is heavily exercised by `tests/test_octodns_manager.py` and interacts with templating behavior (see templating tests) via how processors are applied during planning/dumping.

key_constructs.name: Manager
key_constructs.purpose: Central orchestrator that loads config, instantiates components, and runs sync/dump/compare flows.
key_constructs.reasoning: Most behavioral entry points (including checksum gating, processors/providers wiring, and zone eligibility logic) originate here; tests in `test_octodns_manager.py` instantiate `Manager(...)` and assert counts/exceptions from `sync()`/`dump()`/`compare()`.

key_constructs.name: _AggregateTarget
