key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes strict vs warning behavior so `_process_desired_zone()` can call it consistently for each unsupported scenario.

key_constructs.name: plan
key_constructs.purpose: Orchestrates the provider sync planning workflow end-to-end and returns a Plan or None
key_constructs.reasoning: Defines ordering: populate → desired processing → existing processing → processor passes → compute zone changes → filter changes → add extras → attach meta → build `Plan`/`UnsafePlan`.

key_behaviors: populates existing zone state via populate(..., target=True, lenient=True)
processes desired zone records to omit/transform unsupported types/features
processes existing zone to remove root NS when provider cannot manage it
runs processor hooks in two phases (process_target_zone, then process_source_and_target_zones)
handles backward compatibility for processors not accepting the lenient kwarg
computes zone/record changes via existing.changes(desired, self)
filters computed changes via _include_change
creates a Plan even when only meta changes exist (via _plan_meta)
raises SupportsException for unsupported features when strict_supports=True

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying `desired` or `existing` records in place during `_process_desired_zone` / `_process_existing_zone` rather than using `record.copy()` + `Zone.add_record(..., replace=True)`
pitfalls.consequence: Shallow-copy assumptions break, leading to unintended cross-target pollution or incorrect diffing results
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the documented mutation rules: copy records before changing fields and use Zone replacement/removal APIs.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing method signatures for processor hooks (or removing the lenient kwarg fallback handling) without coordinating across the plugin ecosystem
pitfalls.consequence: Runtime TypeErrors for existing processors that do not accept `lenient`, breaking planning flows
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the existing targeted `TypeError` + message matching behavior in `plan()` for compatibility.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering root NS logic without honoring `root_ns_warnings` and `strict_supports` interactions
pitfalls.consequence: Tests and user expectations diverge: warnings may appear/disappear incorrectly, or exceptions may be raised when only warnings were expected
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain the conditional branches: SUPPORTS_ROOT_NS + configured desired.root_ns + existing_root_ns cleanup + warning verbosity toggle.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming `_plan_meta` is optional-only and that a Plan is returned only when record changes exist
pitfalls.consequence: Consumers may miss plan output changes because meta can trigger plan existence even with zero record changes
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve semantics validated by tests: meta changes can produce a truthy plan and populate `plan.meta`.

key_sections: BaseProvider.plan: planning workflow order, processor hook invocation, lenient fallback compatibility, change filtering and Plan creation
BaseProvider._process_desired_zone: unsupported record handling (supports(), dynamic behavior, pool status/subnet support, multi-value PTR, root NS warnings/handling)
BaseProvider._process_existing_zone: existing root NS cleanup when provider cannot manage it
skip_unless_needed: Default hooks: _include_change, _extra_changes, _plan_meta (only relevant when overriding behavior in providers)

relevant_snippets.description: Defines HelperProvider and TrickyProcessor used to exercise BaseProvider behaviors
test_plan + test_plan_meta validate that meta can cause plan existence and that plan.changes length matches expectations
Processor lenient kwarg fallback tests verify targeted TypeError handling in plan()
test_plan_with_root_ns validates root NS behavior when SUPPORTS_ROOT_NS toggles
test_process_desired_zone validates capability handling for multi-value PTR, dynamic support, pool status, and subnet targeting behavior

related_files.relationship: Direct unit tests for BaseProvider workflow ordering, filtering, root NS, and processor lenient compatibility
related_files.reason_to_check: Any behavioral change in plan sequencing or processing hooks should be validated against these tests.

related_files.relationship: Integration-ish coverage for planning behavior with processors/providers configured via YAML
related_files.reason_to_check: Manager tests may indirectly rely on provider plan/meta output shape and processor hook compatibility behavior.

related_files.relationship: Defines Plan/UnsafePlan constructors and plan output rendering expectations for meta and safety thresholds
related_files.reason_to_check: If `plan()` behavior changes how Plan is constructed or what fields are set, rendering/tests in plan output modules may break.

related_files.relationship: Zone.copy/add_record/remove_record/root_ns/decoded_name and Zone.changes diffing are used heavily by BaseProvider
related_files.reason_to_check: Misunderstanding Zone shallow-copy semantics can break record mutation assumptions relied on by provider preprocessing.

insights.title: Pass lenient mode through provider zone processing
insights.problem: Lenient validation/relaxed modifications needed to propagate consistently through the provider’s existing-zone preprocessing pipeline.
insights.root_cause: Provider base `_process_existing_zone` previously didn’t accept a `lenient` argument, so processors/zone modifications couldn’t reliably enable record leniency for existing zones.
insights.solution: Changed `_process_existing_zone(existing, desired)` to accept `lenient=False` and updated `BaseProvider._populate_and_plan` to call `_process_existing_zone(..., lenient=lenient)`.
insights.lesson_learned: When introducing a new cross-cutting behavior like `lenient`, ensure it propagates through both desired and existing processing paths (not just one).

insights.title: Pass lenient through processor hooks with backward-compatible fallbacks
insights.problem: Older processors/providers may not accept new `lenient` kwargs; passing them unconditionally would break compatibility and crash at runtime.
insights.root_cause: Lenient parameter support required changing method signatures across processors and providers, but the ecosystem may include processors that only implement older method signatures.
insights.solution: Updated `octodns/manager.py` and `BaseProvider.plan()` plumbing to try calling processor methods with `lenient`, catch `TypeError` for unexpected keyword, log deprecation warnings, and fall back to calling without `lenient` (only for the specific mismatch).
insights.lesson_learned: For public plugin-like extension points, implement compatibility fallbacks around signature expansion; avoid catching broad exceptions—match on `TypeError` + message content to prevent masking real bugs.

insights.title: Make root NS warnings suppressible and respect strict_supports
insights.problem: Providers emitting root NS-related warnings/behavior couldn't be disabled, and the response when root NS is unsupported did not consistently follow strict_supports vs warning behavior.
insights.root_cause: BaseProvider hard-coded warning/exception behavior around SUPPORTS_ROOT_NS and desired.root_ns presence without a provider-level toggle to suppress warnings.
insights.solution: Added new BaseProvider constructor parameter `root_ns_warnings` (default True). Updated `_process_desired_zone` and the later info log for existing root NS to only log when `root_ns_warnings` is enabled. Also updated logic: when `strict_supports` is enabled and root NS is unsupported, raise `SupportsException`; otherwise warn (if `root_ns_warnings`).
insights.lesson_learned: When adding provider capabilities/constraints behavior, ensure both warning verbosity and strict mode are independently controllable so downstream users can reduce noise without losing safety guarantees.

insights.title: Root NS cases are covered across warning toggles + strict mode
insights.problem: Risk of regressions in nuanced combinations of SUPPORTS_ROOT_NS / configured root NS / strict_supports / warning toggles.
insights.root_cause: Previously, tests likely did not cover the full Cartesian product of root NS support and log suppression.
insights.solution: Introduced `TestBaseProvider.test_root_ns_warnings` asserting warnings logged (or not) for each scenario, and that SupportsException is raised when strict_supports=True.
insights.lesson_learned: For capability-dependent behavior (support flags vs configured records), tests should cover combinations, not only the “happy path”.

insights.title: Plan now supports provider meta changes (non-record changes) and affects plan output schema
insights.problem: Existing assumptions about Plan having only record changes may break consumers that parse plan output/JSON schema or render logs expecting only `changes` key.
insights.root_cause: New `BaseProvider._plan_meta` and propagation to `Plan(meta=...)` introduces additional data in `Plan.data` and plan renderers, changing the serialized shape.
insights.solution: Added `BaseProvider._plan_meta(existing, desired, changes)` hook, passed `meta` into `Plan`, updated `Plan.data` to include `'meta'`, and updated log/markdown/html renderers to display meta when present.
insights.lesson_learned: When extending plan/output models, update tests and any downstream consumers’ schema expectations; prefer opaque dict meta with pprint-friendly requirements.

octodns/cmds/report.py.short_description: CLI command that queries DNS resolvers for a configured zone and outputs CSV/JSON reports.
octodns/cmds/report.py.description:
This file implements the `octodns-report`-style CLI entry point that produces a per-record DNS consistency report by actively querying one or more DNS resolvers. It loads the OctoDNS `Manager` configuration, populates a target `Zone` with records from the selected `source` providers, then schedules asynchronous DNS lookups for every `(record, resolver)` pair. Results are aggregated into an in-memory structure and emitted as either CSV (with a “Consistent” column) or JSON (with a “consistent” boolean).

The key behavioral design choice is the async query fan-out with explicit concurrency limiting. The `async_resolve` coroutine creates a dnspython `AsyncResolver` configured with the provided nameserver (single resolver per query), sets a lifetime timeout, and maps DNS outcomes to deterministic sentinel strings: `*no answer*`, `*does not exist*`, `*should not exist*`, and `*timeout*`. These placeholders are then used to decide consistency per record across resolvers by comparing lowercased answer strings (CSV) or joined values (JSON).

Another important integration point is how resolver inputs are interpreted. The CLI accepts `server` arguments that can be IPv4/IPv6 addresses or hostnames. For IPs it normalizes IPv6 via `ip_address(...).compressed`. For hostnames it resolves the hostname to an IP by trying `A` then `AAAA` lookups using dnspython’s synchronous `resolve()` and uses the first RR found; failures are swallowed broadly to allow trying other rrtypes. The output includes one column/key per unique normalized resolver address and relies on stable ordering via the `resolvers` list construction and `sorted(zone.records)` / `sorted(queries.items())`.

When modifying this file, preserve its current CLI contract (argument names and meanings), its concurrency/timeout semantics, and the exact output conventions used to determine “consistency” (case-insensitive equality on the rendered answer content). Also note that this file does not appear to be covered by any tests included in the provided context, so manual/CLI-level verification may be necessary when changing logic around DNS resolution, aggregation, or formatting.

key_constructs.name: async_resolve
key_constructs.purpose: Asynchronously query a single record type/name against one resolver with concurrency limiting and sentinel mapping.
key_constructs.reasoning: This coroutine is the unit of concurrency: it must return a tuple `[record, resolver, answer_list]` so the caller can aggregate by record and resolver. Changes here directly affect output correctness and the consistency computation.

key_constructs.name: main
key_constructs.purpose: Parse CLI args, load manager sources into a zone, build resolver list, schedule DNS query tasks, and emit CSV/JSON output.
key_constructs.reasoning: This function orchestrates the end-to-end report lifecycle: CLI parsing, zone population via providers, DNS task creation and loop execution, result aggregation, and final output formatting.

key_behaviors: Loads OctoDNS Manager config and resolves `sources` by name from `manager.providers`
Populates a Zone by calling `source.populate(zone, lenient=...)` for each selected provider
Normalizes resolver CLI inputs into a unique `resolvers` list (IPv6 compressed or first A/AAAA IP from hostname)
Creates asyncio tasks for each record/resolver pair and enforces a global concurrency limit via Semaphore
Performs dnspython async DNS queries with lifetime timeout and maps specific dnspython exceptions to sentinel strings
Aggregates results into a nested structure keyed by record decoded fqdn and resolver
Emits CSV with columns: Name, Type, TTL, one column per resolver, and Consistent
Emits JSON where each record/type has resolver answer arrays and a `consistent` boolean

pitfalls.mistake: Changing sentinel strings or their capitalization without updating downstream consistency logic.
pitfalls.consequence: “Consistent” calculations may change meaning or break expected output formats for consumers relying on specific placeholders.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat the sentinel values (`*no answer*`, `*does not exist*`, `*should not exist*`, `*timeout*`) as part of the output contract.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering how resolver uniqueness/ordering is computed (e.g., normalized IPv6 form or hostname-to-IP selection).
pitfalls.consequence: CSV/JSON headers/keys may differ run-to-run, and records may be treated as queried against different resolver identities.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve normalization behavior (`ip.compressed` for IPv6 and “first RR found” for hostnames) and the uniqueness check.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or bypassing the concurrency Semaphore around `AsyncResolver.resolve()` calls.
pitfalls.consequence: Unbounded parallel DNS queries can overwhelm resolver services or local resources, affecting reliability and timing.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the `async with limit:` structure as the mechanism controlling query fan-out rate.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying the consistency check normalization rules (CSV uses `answer.lower()` keys; JSON uses `' '.join(answer).lower()`).
pitfalls.consequence: Whitespace/quoting differences (especially TXT) can change consistency outcomes or consumer parsing expectations.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect current normalization and the TXT quote stripping behavior in JSON output.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing JSON output structure keys (`record.decoded_fqdn` vs `record.fqdn`, and where `consistent` is placed).
pitfalls.consequence: Downstream tools may fail because of mismatched schema shape or inconsistent naming.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain the current schema: JSON uses `jsonout[record.decoded_fqdn][record._type][resolver] = answer` and then sets `jsonout[record.fqdn][record._type]['consistent']`.

key_sections: main: CLI argument parsing and creation of Manager/zone/sources
main: resolver argument normalization (ip_address compression and hostname A/AAAA resolution)
main: asyncio setup (new_event_loop, Semaphore, task fan-out over sorted(zone.records) and resolvers)
main: aggregation into `queries` and output formatting branches for csv/json
async_resolve: dnspython async query with timeout and exception-to-sentinel mapping
skip_unless_needed: imports at top-level
the small CSV header construction details unless touching formatting

related_files.relationship: Provides `Manager(args.config_file)`, `manager.providers`, and `get_zone(args.zone)` used to load sources and records for reporting.
related_files.reason_to_check: If records’ attributes (`decoded_fqdn`, `_type`, `ttl`, `fqdn`) or provider population behavior change, report output may need to be kept consistent.

related_files.relationship: Defines `ArgumentParser` used for consistent CLI help/behavior.
related_files.reason_to_check: If argument parsing or default behaviors differ across CLI commands, this report command’s parsing contract can be impacted.

insights.title: Handle missing resolver answers safely using default []
insights.problem: When a resolver produced no entries for a record/type, code attempted to join or iterate over None and could raise errors or emit invalid output.
insights.root_cause: Use of answers.get(resolver) without a default can return None when the resolver key is missing.
insights.solution: When formatting CSV and JSON, use answers.get(resolver, []) so joins/loops operate on an empty list.
insights.lesson_learned: When aggregating async results into dicts, missing keys are common. Always decide whether you want 'missing == empty' and encode that explicitly with defaults.

insights.title: Fix resolver membership check and make exception handling explicit
insights.problem: Resolver hostname/IP filtering and exception handling were either too broad or incorrect, leading to inconsistent logging/output behavior.
insights.root_cause: (1) The code used `if resolver and not resolver in resolvers:`; while logically OK, it was later corrected to a clearer `resolver not in resolvers`. (2) It used bare `except:` during DNS resolution; this can mask unrelated errors.
insights.solution: (1) Change to `if resolver and resolver not in resolvers:`. (2) Narrow `except:` to `except Exception:` in the area that ignores NXDOMAIN/NoAnswer-style cases.
insights.lesson_learned: Avoid bare `except:` in network/async code; explicitly catch only what you mean. Also prefer clear, idiomatic containment checks to reduce future mistakes.

insights.title: Rewrite octodns-report to use asyncio + concurrency limiting and async DNS resolution
insights.problem: The prior implementation used thread-based async and was being expanded to support IPv6 resolvers, async resolution, and JSON output; concurrency control and efficiency needed improvement.
insights.root_cause: ThreadPoolExecutor-based approach was less direct for async DNS workloads and made it harder to implement a global concurrency limiter cleanly.
insights.solution: Implement async_resolve(record, resolver, timeout, limit) using dnspython's async resolver, a Semaphore to limit concurrency, and aggregate results into CSV/JSON output formats.
insights.lesson_learned: For high-rate DNS querying, prefer an async model with an explicit concurrency semaphore. Keep error-to-placeholder mapping deterministic (*timeout*, *does not exist*, etc.) so output consumers can rely on stable sentinel values.

insights.title: Support IPv6 resolvers by compressing IP form and resolving hostnames
insights.problem: Resolvers could be specified as hostnames or IPv6 addresses, and parsing needed to normalize IPv6 while still allowing hostname resolution to IPs.
insights.root_cause: Naive resolver parsing would treat IPv6 forms as non-IP strings and fail to configure dnspython properly.
insights.solution: Use ipaddress.ip_address(server) and `ip.compressed` to normalize IPv6; for non-IP inputs, attempt A/AAAA resolution and use the first found RR.
insights.lesson_learned: When a parameter accepts multiple input types (hostname or IP), handle IPv4/IPv6 explicitly and normalize to a canonical representation before use.

octodns/processor/clamp.py.short_description: Processor that clamps record TTLs to a configured min/max range.
octodns/processor/clamp.py.description:
This module defines a processor responsible for enforcing a TTL range on desired Zone records produced during provider/source processing. It exists to be plugged into octoDNS’ processor pipeline via configuration (as shown in the class docstring), where it will iterate over `desired.records` and adjust each record’s `ttl` in-place when it falls outside the configured bounds.

The core invariant is validated at initialization: `min_ttl` must be less than or equal to `max_ttl`. If the range is logically invalid, the processor raises a dedicated `TTLArgumentException` (a subclass of `ProcessorException`) immediately, ensuring deterministic failure for invalid processor configuration rather than ambiguous behavior during processing.

During `process_source_zone`, the processor clamps each record TTL using `max(min_ttl, min(max_ttl, original_ttl))`. It logs when clamping occurs, including the record’s `fqdn`, `_type`, and the before/after TTL values. The method signature includes `desired`, `sources`, and `lenient`, but this processor does not use `sources` or `lenient`, indicating it applies uniformly regardless of the origin(s) and is expected to be compatible with the generic processor interface.

In the broader system, `TtlClampProcessor` participates in the zone transformation pipeline under `octodns/processor/*`, where processors are invoked by the manager/context machinery to transform a desired Zone before planning/applying changes.

key_constructs.name: TTLArgumentException
key_constructs.purpose: Signals invalid TTL-related processor configuration (min/max mismatch).
key_constructs.reasoning: Having a dedicated exception type allows callers/config loaders to distinguish configuration errors from other processor failures.

key_constructs.name: TtlClampProcessor
key_constructs.purpose: Clamps record TTLs in a zone to the inclusive range [min_ttl, max_ttl].
key_constructs.reasoning: Implements octoDNS processor interface with `process_source_zone`, making it deployable via config under `processors:`.

key_constructs.name: process_source_zone
key_constructs.purpose: Iterates records in `desired` and updates each record TTL if outside bounds.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the behavior tested and relied upon by the processor pipeline; it performs in-place mutation and returns the same `desired` object.

key_behaviors: validates min_ttl <= max_ttl during __init__
iterates desired.records and computes clamped TTL per record
mutates record.ttl in-place when clamping is needed
logs an info message for each TTL change

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or weakening the `min_ttl` <= `max_ttl` validation
pitfalls.consequence: Invalid ranges could cause clamping to behave incorrectly or yield surprising TTLs during processing rather than failing fast at config load/startup.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the initialization check and keep raising `TTLArgumentException` for invalid argument combinations.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the clamping semantics from inclusive boundaries
pitfalls.consequence: Records with TTL exactly equal to `min_ttl` or `max_ttl` may be incorrectly altered, breaking expected behavior and tests.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure the clamp formula remains inclusive (current logic clamps into [min_ttl, max_ttl]).

pitfalls.mistake: Changing return/value semantics of `process_source_zone` (e.g., creating a new Zone vs in-place mutation)
pitfalls.consequence: Other parts of the pipeline or tests may depend on receiving the processed `desired` object reflecting TTL changes; returning a different object can impact identity/equality expectations.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect that `process_source_zone` currently mutates `desired` in place and returns it.

key_sections: TtlClampProcessor.__init__: validation and storing min/max TTL bounds
TtlClampProcessor.process_source_zone: record iteration and clamping logic
skip_unless_needed: TTLArgumentException class body (no additional logic)

relevant_snippets.description: Imports and wiring of TTLArgumentException/TtlClampProcessor with Zone/Record.
test_processor_min: constructs a Zone with too-low TTL and asserts processed record TTL equals min_ttl.
test_processor_max: constructs a Zone with too-high TTL and asserts processed record TTL equals max_ttl.
test_processor_maxmin: asserts invalid configuration (min_ttl > max_ttl) raises TTLArgumentException.
test_processor_minmax: asserts both lower and upper clamp cases are applied correctly.
test_processor_noclamp: asserts TTL within range remains unchanged.

related_files.relationship: Unit tests define the expected clamping and configuration-validation behavior for this processor.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any behavioral change to clamping bounds, mutation/return semantics, or exception type must keep these tests passing.

insights.title: Raise specific TTLArgumentException when min/max TTL arguments are not logical
insights.problem: The processor accepted invalid TTL configuration where min_ttl could be >= max_ttl, leading to incorrect clamping behavior or misleading results.
insights.root_cause: Missing validation that `min_ttl` must be strictly less than or equal to `max_ttl` (checked as `not min_ttl <= max_ttl`) before using the range.
insights.solution: Added `TTLArgumentException(ProcessorException)` and raised it in `TtlClampProcessor.__init__` when `min_ttl` is not <= `max_ttl`.
insights.lesson_learned: Validate configuration invariants at processor initialization so failures are deterministic and attributable to user config.

insights.title: Switch logger setup and log formatting to printf-style
insights.problem: Inconsistent logger naming and eager f-string formatting produced less consistent logs and potentially more overhead than necessary.
insights.root_cause: Use of f-strings in log message construction and logger naming included module path unnecessarily.
insights.solution: Changed logger initialization from `f'{module}.{class}'` to `getLogger(self.__class__.__name__)` and replaced several `f'...'` log messages with `%s` / argument-based logging.
insights.lesson_learned: Prefer parameterized logging (`logger.info('...', %s)`) to avoid eager string formatting and keep log formatting consistent.

tests/test_octodns_processor_clamp.py.short_description: Unit tests covering TTL clamping behavior and invalid configuration handling.
tests/test_octodns_processor_clamp.py.description:
This file contains unit tests for `TtlClampProcessor` and its `TTLArgumentException`. The tests construct minimal `Zone` and `Record` objects using `Record.new(...)` and validate that `process_source_zone` clamps TTLs into the configured inclusive range.

The suite exercises both boundary-direction cases (too low -> becomes min, too high -> becomes max), the combined scenario with multiple records in the same zone (asserting correct lower/upper clamping independent of record order), and the no-op scenario where TTL is already within bounds (asserting TTL remains unchanged). A dedicated test validates that an invalid configuration where `min_ttl > max_ttl` raises `TTLArgumentException` at processor instantiation.

The tests rely on `Zone.copy()` when calling `process_source_zone`, and they compare record TTLs from `processed_zone`. They also assert `self.assertNotEqual(zone, processed_zone)`, implying that zone equality semantics are meaningful here (likely incorporating record TTL differences after processing).

key_constructs.name: TestClampProcessor
key_constructs.purpose: Groups all unit tests for clamp processor behavior.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures consistent setup patterns and uses unittest assertions to validate outcomes.

key_constructs.name: test_processor_min
key_constructs.purpose: Verifies TTL below min_ttl is clamped up to min_ttl.
key_constructs.reasoning: Locks in lower-bound clamping semantics and in-place effect observable via returned Zone.

key_constructs.name: test_processor_max
key_constructs.purpose: Verifies TTL above max_ttl is clamped down to max_ttl.
key_constructs.reasoning: Locks in upper-bound clamping semantics.

key_constructs.name: test_processor_maxmin
key_constructs.purpose: Verifies invalid min/max configuration raises TTLArgumentException.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures failure happens at initialization and uses the expected exception type.

key_constructs.name: test_processor_minmax
key_constructs.purpose: Verifies both lower and upper clamping occur in one zone with multiple records.
key_constructs.reasoning: Prevents accidental logic that only clamps one direction or depends on record iteration order.

key_constructs.name: test_processor_noclamp
key_constructs.purpose: Verifies TTL already in range remains unchanged.
key_constructs.reasoning: Prevents over-clamping or off-by-one boundary errors.

key_behaviors: constructs a Zone and Record with specific TTLs
calls TtlClampProcessor.process_source_zone on zone.copy()
asserts clamped TTL values for too-low/too-high scenarios
asserts TTL unchanged when already within range
asserts TTLArgumentException on invalid processor configuration

pitfalls.mistake: Changing how Zone.copy() or Zone equality works without updating expectations in these tests
pitfalls.consequence: The `assertNotEqual(zone, processed_zone)` assertions may fail even if TTL clamping is correct, depending on equality semantics.
pitfalls.prevention: If modifying core Zone equality/copy semantics, re-check these tests’ equality assumptions.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering inclusivity of bounds in the processor
pitfalls.consequence: These tests use strictly inside/outside values, but off-by-one changes could still break boundary cases in future added tests or existing ones if assumptions change.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep processor’s clamp formula consistent with test expectations for values chosen around min/max.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the exception type raised on invalid min/max arguments
pitfalls.consequence: test_processor_maxmin will fail if a different exception type is raised.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure invalid-configuration failures keep raising `TTLArgumentException`.

test_processor_max: sets too_high_ttl > max_ttl then asserts processed TTL == max_ttl.

octodns/provider/plan.py.short_description: Implements Plan safety checks and renderers for JSON/Markdown/HTML/log outputs.
octodns/provider/plan.py.description:
This module defines the core “Plan” object used by the provider planning/apply workflow. A Plan captures the intended changes between an existing Zone state and a desired Zone state, along with whether the provider should be allowed to proceed (“exists” indicates whether the zone already exists). The Plan also enforces safety constraints via raise_if_unsafe(), preventing excessive update/delete operations and special-casing the root NS record change as always unsafe.

Beyond safety, this module provides multiple plan output renderers: PlanLogger, PlanJson, PlanMarkdown, and PlanHtml. These classes are designed to be called with an iterable of (target, plan) pairs; each renderer emits human-readable or machine-readable representations while iterating plans in a deterministic order (the renderer groups output by zone using plan.desired.decoded_name). The output layer supports either writing to an already-provided file handle (fh) or writing to output_filename, using the _custom_fh decorator to manage file handle creation/closure.

Key design constraints that callers/tests rely on include: Plan.changes is sorted at construction time (deterministic ordering); Plan.data is a serialization-friendly dict containing a list of each change’s .data and the optional meta; renderers format “Create” rows differently when plan.exists is False; and safety thresholds are derived from either Plan constructor parameters or zone/provider thresholds present on existing (with zone precedence when existing has non-None thresholds).

key_constructs.name: Plan
key_constructs.purpose: Holds existing/desired state, computed change list, safety thresholds, and metadata; validates plan safety.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the central abstraction tested heavily: it sorts changes deterministically, derives thresholds with precedence rules, provides a stable serialization surface (data), and enforces unsafe-operation constraints via raise_if_unsafe().

key_constructs.name: Plan.raise_if_unsafe
key_constructs.purpose: Raises TooMuchChange or RootNsChange when update/delete counts exceed configured safe limits or root NS is modified.
key_constructs.reasoning: This method is the enforcement point used by higher-level apply/confirm flows; tests validate thresholds, min-existing gating, and special root NS detection.

key_constructs.name: Plan.data
key_constructs.purpose: Returns a dict with 'changes' (list of change.data) and 'meta' for serialization/checksums.
key_constructs.reasoning: Tests assert exact keys and structure, and other parts of the system may rely on deterministic serialization for checksumming or plan output.

key_constructs.name: TooMuchChange
key_constructs.purpose: UnsafePlan subclass carrying a formatted error message when too many updates/deletes are detected.
key_constructs.reasoning: The exception message is used in tests via substring checks ('Too many updates' / 'Too many deletes'). Preserve message wording semantics when changing logic.

key_constructs.name: RootNsChange
key_constructs.purpose: UnsafePlan subclass used when the zone’s root NS (name '') is changed.
key_constructs.reasoning: Tests expect RootNsChange to be raised when any NS change targets the root (record._type == 'NS' and record.name == '').

key_constructs.name: PlanLogger
key_constructs.purpose: Renders a textual, sectioned plan summary to a logger at a configurable level.
key_constructs.reasoning: Tests verify unsupported log levels and that the output includes an exact summary string for created/updated/deleted counts.

key_constructs.name: PlanJson
key_constructs.purpose: Renders plans as JSON, grouped by target.id then plan.desired.name, including change.data and meta.
key_constructs.reasoning: JSON structure and inclusion of top-level keys are asserted in tests; output_filename behavior is covered by file-writing tests.

key_constructs.name: PlanMarkdown
key_constructs.purpose: Renders plans as Markdown tables, including create/update/delete rows and optional meta.
key_constructs.reasoning: Tests assert presence of specific table row fragments and geo/value stringification.

key_constructs.name: PlanHtml
key_constructs.purpose: Renders plans as HTML tables with create/update/delete rows and optional meta.
key_constructs.reasoning: Tests assert empty-plan output and a specific summary row fragment for non-empty plans; it is sensitive to exact HTML text.

key_constructs.name: _custom_fh
key_constructs.purpose: Decorator that, when output_filename is set, opens the file and injects fh into the render method call.
key_constructs.reasoning: All Plan* renderers rely on this behavior to satisfy the output_filename-related tests; changing its contract impacts multiple renderers.

key_constructs.name: _value_stringifier
key_constructs.purpose: Formats record values, including geo values, into a string joined by a provided separator.
key_constructs.reasoning: Markdown/HTML renderers call this to format both existing and new record values; tests check for geo formatting in Markdown.

key_behaviors: Sorts changes deterministically at Plan construction via sorted(changes)
Computes create/update/delete counts from the change class names
Uses existing zone thresholds when existing.update_pcent_threshold / delete_pcent_threshold are non-None; otherwise falls back to constructor thresholds
raise_if_unsafe enforces max update/delete percentage when existing.records length >= MIN_EXISTING_RECORDS
Min-existing-record gating: if existing.records length < MIN_EXISTING_RECORDS, safety thresholds are not applied
Counts only Update and Delete toward thresholds; Create does not count
Forces unsafe via RootNsChange when any change targets an NS record with record.name == '' (root NS record)
Plan.data produces deterministic serialization-friendly dict with 'changes' and 'meta'
Renderers group output by zone using plan.desired.decoded_name changes between targets
JSON groups by target.id then plan.desired.name and includes plan.data
When plan.exists is False, renderers include a 'Create' row/section for the desired record

pitfalls.mistake: Altering the structure or key names returned by Plan.data
pitfalls.consequence: JSON output tests and downstream checksum/integrity consumers may break due to missing/changed keys ('changes' and 'meta') or list structure.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve Plan.data schema and ordering/structure expectations that tests assert.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing safety threshold precedence semantics between zone thresholds and provider/constructor thresholds
pitfalls.consequence: TestPlanSafety.test_too_many_zone_updates/deletes will fail; apply safety behavior may differ from expected policy.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain the precedence rule: non-None thresholds from existing (zone) override Plan constructor threshold parameters.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying Root NS detection logic or the specific conditions used
pitfalls.consequence: TestPlanSafety.test_root_ns_change will fail and unsafe enforcement may not trigger for root NS modifications.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the root NS predicate aligned to c.record._type == 'NS' and c.record.name == ''.

pitfalls.mistake: Breaking output_filename behavior by changing _custom_fh contract
pitfalls.consequence: All output_filename tests for JSON/Markdown/HTML will fail; file handles may not be correctly managed.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure render methods still accept fh and _custom_fh still injects it when output_filename is set.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering exact output strings used in tests (HTML summary cell, Markdown table row fragments, Logger summary)
pitfalls.consequence: String containment tests will fail even if semantics are equivalent.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat textual formatting in PlanLogger/PlanMarkdown/PlanHtml as a compatibility surface for these tests.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing how geo values are stringified
pitfalls.consequence: Markdown/HTML tests and human expectations for geo output may fail (e.g., 'NA-US: 6.6.6.6').
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve _value_stringifier’s behavior: str(v) conversion, geo sorting by code, and separator joining.

Plan.raise_if_unsafe: update/delete ratio enforcement and RootNsChange detection
Plan.data: serialization surface used by JSON renderer and tests
_custom_fh + PlanJson/PlanMarkdown/PlanHtml: output_filename file writing behavior and text formatting

PlanLogger.run details if not touching logger rendering

related_files.reason_to_check: Any behavioral or string-format changes in Plan or renderers will likely require aligning expected outputs or updating safety thresholds tests.

insights.title: Rename output_path to output_filename and refactor plan outputs with decorator for custom fh
insights.problem: Plan output classes previously used `output_path`, and later requirements/issue likely clarified desired naming/behavior; refactor also reduces repeated `try/finally` close logic.
insights.root_cause: Original implementation scattered file-handle management per output class with `should_close` logic and used a parameter name (`output_path`) that needed renaming.
insights.solution: Introduced `_PlanFhOutput` and `_custom_fh` decorator. Renamed constructor arg from `output_path` to `output_filename`, so when set, it opens the file and injects `fh` into `run()`; closure is handled by the decorator’s `with open(...)` scope. Updated tests accordingly.
insights.lesson_learned: When multiple classes share identical file-handle handling patterns, centralize it (e.g., decorator) to reduce bugs and ensure consistent resource management. Keep parameter naming aligned with user-facing expectations and update tests.

insights.title: output_path -> output_filename for plan output classes
insights.problem: Code/configs using `output_path` would break once renamed to `output_filename`.
insights.root_cause: Explicit parameter rename and refactor of `_PlanOutput` init signature.
insights.solution: Refactored constructors/tests to use `output_filename` and updated changelog accordingly. (No compatibility shim is shown in this diff, so treat as breaking for users relying on old config keys.)
insights.lesson_learned: Parameter renames in user-facing config should be treated as breaking changes; ideally include backward compatible aliasing or clear release notes.

insights.title: Plan serialization and renderers now include provider meta
insights.problem: Consumers of Plan output might not expect `meta` key or meta sections in HTML/Markdown.
insights.root_cause: Plan now stores `meta` and updates renderers to include it when present.
insights.solution: Added `meta` parameter to `Plan`, included it in `Plan.data`, and extended PlanLogger/PlanMarkdown/PlanHtml to print meta using `pprint.pformat`.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding output fields, update both JSON schema expectations and human-readable renderers/tests together.

insights.title: Plan threshold selection: cleanly apply zone precedence
insights.problem: Threshold values could be overridden incorrectly depending on existing/zone/provider precedence handling.
insights.root_cause: Refactor aimed to make explicit assignment order between `existing` zone thresholds and provider defaults/explicit config.
insights.solution: Adjusted initialization so that when `existing` provides non-None thresholds, they override; otherwise provider parameters are used (with explicit else branches).
insights.lesson_learned: When multiple config layers exist (provider vs zone), encode precedence explicitly with clear control flow and tests around precedence.

insights.title: Expose Plan.data for deterministic checksum serialization
insights.problem: Checksum feature needed a stable, serialization-friendly view of the plan.
insights.root_cause: Prior to checksum support, plan/change objects didn’t provide a uniform dict representation for integrity hashing.
insights.solution: Added Plan.data property returning {'changes': [c.data ...]} to provide structured, consistent data for hashing and tests.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding integrity checks, provide explicit serialization formats rather than relying on object repr/implicit serialization.

tests/test_octodns_plan.py.short_description: Unit tests covering Plan safety checks, serialization, and renderer output formats.
tests/test_octodns_plan.py.description:
This test module exercises the behavior of octodns/provider/plan.py, using real Record/Change objects plus a SimpleProvider target-like object. It validates three broad areas: (1) Plan determinism by asserting changes are sorted in Plan.changes; (2) Plan safety logic via raise_if_unsafe() including update/delete ratio thresholds, min-existing gating, zone threshold precedence, and Root NS record enforcement; and (3) renderer outputs (logger/JSON/Markdown/HTML) both when writing to an in-memory fh and when using output_filename.

The module builds a shared set of changes and plans at import time, then uses them across tests to verify exact key presence in JSON output, presence of specific rendered fragments in HTML/Markdown output, and the summary line content for logger output. Safety tests use a HelperPlan subclass to override MIN_EXISTING_RECORDS, allowing threshold enforcement behavior to be tested for both low and high existing record counts.

The test suite is string/structure sensitive: it asserts exact exception message substrings ('Too many updates'/'Too many deletes' and 'Root NS record change'), exact Plan.data top-level keys ordering, and exact HTML/summary snippets embedded in renderer output. Any edits to plan.py that change formatting or schema can break these tests.

key_constructs.reasoning: The plan constructor sorts changes via sorted(changes); this test confirms ordering is not dependent on input order.

key_constructs.reasoning: HTML output is asserted via contains checks and exact match for empty state.

key_constructs.reasoning: Uses json.loads and navigates nested dictionaries by key sequences.

key_constructs.reasoning: Checks rendered table rows/values using substring assertions.

key_constructs.name: HelperPlan

key_constructs.reasoning: Enables testing threshold enforcement even with fewer existing records.

key_constructs.name: TestPlanSafety

key_constructs.reasoning: Covers multiple combinations of existing record counts and change types.

key_constructs.purpose: Tests output_filename behavior for PlanJson/PlanMarkdown/PlanHtml and fh default behavior.
key_constructs.reasoning: Asserts correct file contents are produced when renderer opens a file.

octodns/processor/filter.py.short_description: DNS zone record filter processors for include/allow/reject and plan validation.
octodns/processor/filter.py.description:
This module defines a set of processor classes used by octodns to filter records from zones during reconciliation, and to optionally validate/remove certain plan changes. Processors operate in the context of octodns’ pipeline (source normalization vs target reconciliation), typically mutating a Zone by removing records that do not match configured criteria. The design centralizes record-removal semantics via mixins and shared base processors, so allow/deny variants can share the same iteration and decision flow.

A central abstraction is `_FilterProcessor`, which provides `process_source_zone` and `process_target_zone` and gates whether the target zone is filtered using `include_target` (defaulting to True). This flag is significant: many allowlist/rejectlist processors are expected to behave differently depending on whether the processor is being applied while computing desired/source normalization or while reconciling against existing/target state. The mixins `AllowsMixin` and `RejectsMixin` implement `matches`/`doesnt_match` behavior by either removing or keeping records; in combination with base classes, allow vs reject filters consistently translate match results into keep/remove outcomes.

The file also implements pattern-based filtering for record types (`TypeAllowlistFilter` / `TypeRejectlistFilter`), record names (`NameAllowlistFilter` / `NameRejectlistFilter`), record values (`ValueAllowlistFilter` / `ValueRejectlistFilter`), and A/AAAA record values interpreted as IPs against CIDR allow/reject lists (`NetworkValueAllowlistFilter` / `NetworkValueRejectlistFilter`). Additionally, it defines safety/guard processors for NS root records: `IgnoreRootNsFilter` removes root NS records during zone processing, and `ExcludeRootNsChanges` enforces that root NS changes are disallowed in plans (either raising `ValidationError` or removing disallowed changes depending on `error`). These processors integrate with `octodns.record.exception.ValidationError` and `octodns.provider.plan.Plan` by manipulating `plan.changes` based on record attributes.

When editing this module, preserve the matching semantics around what constitutes “match” for each filter category. Particularly, name/type matching uses exact and compiled regex lists, while value matching uses `value.rdata_text` (including multi-valued records) and logs warnings for `None` values. Network value matching converts configured CIDRs at construction time (raising on invalid CIDR input) and only considers record types `A` and `AAAA` (leaving other record types untouched). These semantics are exercised by the unit tests and likely relied upon by downstream processor configuration in real usage.

key_constructs.name: _FilterProcessor
key_constructs.purpose: Base class for processors that filter records in source vs target zones with include_target gating.
key_constructs.reasoning: The `include_target` flag controls whether target-zone records are removed. Tests explicitly cover scenarios where include_target=False leaves existing target records intact (so delete operations can still occur elsewhere). Any edits must maintain this contract to avoid changing reconciliation outcomes.

key_constructs.name: AllowsMixin
key_constructs.purpose: Implements allowlist semantics for matching/non-matching records by removing non-matches.
key_constructs.reasoning: The `matches` method is intentionally a no-op, while `doesnt_match` removes the record from the zone. This pattern is relied on by multiple allowlist processors through multiple inheritance.

key_constructs.name: RejectsMixin
key_constructs.purpose: Implements rejectlist semantics for matching/non-matching records by removing matches.
key_constructs.reasoning: The `matches` method removes the record from the zone, while `doesnt_match` is a no-op. This inversion is key for correct allow vs reject behavior in shared base classes.

key_constructs.name: _TypeBaseFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Base implementation for filtering records based on record._type membership in a configured set.
key_constructs.reasoning: It loops through `zone.records` and delegates keep/remove to `matches`/`doesnt_match` based on `record._type`. Edits must preserve iteration/removal behavior (mutating zone during iteration) to keep test outcomes stable.

key_constructs.name: TypeAllowlistFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Allowlist processor that keeps only records whose _type is present in allowlist.
key_constructs.reasoning: Inherits `_TypeBaseFilter` and `AllowsMixin`, so match results should keep records and non-matches should be removed. The constructor wiring to `allowlist` and passthrough of `include_target` is part of the public processor configuration surface.

key_constructs.name: TypeRejectlistFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Rejectlist processor that removes records whose _type is present in rejectlist.
key_constructs.reasoning: Inherits `_TypeBaseFilter` and `RejectsMixin`, which removes matching records. Tests cover source vs target behavior and ensure only specified types are filtered.

key_constructs.name: _NameBaseFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Base implementation for filtering records based on exact name match or regex patterns.
key_constructs.reasoning: It partitions configured patterns into exact string matches and regex (for entries starting with '/'). The regex compilation strips the leading/trailing '/'. Behavior must remain consistent with tests using patterns like '/^start-.+-end$/'

key_constructs.name: NameAllowlistFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Allowlist processor that keeps records whose record.name matches configured exact/regex patterns.
key_constructs.reasoning: Uses `_NameBaseFilter` plus `AllowsMixin`, meaning non-matching records are removed.

key_constructs.name: NameRejectlistFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Rejectlist processor that removes records whose record.name matches configured exact/regex patterns.
key_constructs.reasoning: Uses `_NameBaseFilter` plus `RejectsMixin`, meaning matching records are removed.

key_constructs.name: _ValueBaseFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Base implementation for filtering records based on record values (single value or multi-value) matched against exact/regex patterns.
key_constructs.reasoning: It derives `values` from `record.values` (multi) via `value.rdata_text` or from `record.value` via `record.value.rdata_text`. If neither exists, it warns and implicitly treats it as no-match, which is tested via `lenient=True` records with `value=None`.

key_constructs.name: ValueAllowlistFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Allowlist processor that keeps records whose values match configured exact/regex patterns.
key_constructs.reasoning: Initializes a filter-specific logger and relies on `_ValueBaseFilter` plus `AllowsMixin` for removal of non-matches.

key_constructs.name: ValueRejectlistFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Rejectlist processor that removes records whose values match configured exact/regex patterns.
key_constructs.reasoning: Initializes a filter-specific logger and relies on `_ValueBaseFilter` plus `RejectsMixin` for removal of matches.

key_constructs.name: _NetworkValueBaseFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Base implementation for filtering A/AAAA records based on whether each record’s IP values fall within configured CIDR networks.
key_constructs.reasoning: Constructor validates CIDR strings using `ip_network(value)` and stores `self.networks`. Processing converts each `record.values` entry with `ip_address`, then checks membership across the cartesian product of record IPs and configured networks using `product(ips, self.networks)`.

key_constructs.name: NetworkValueAllowlistFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Allowlist processor for A/AAAA records where IP values must be inside one of the configured CIDR networks.
key_constructs.reasoning: Inherits `_NetworkValueBaseFilter` and `AllowsMixin`, so only A/AAAA with in-network values are kept; other A/AAAA are removed and all other record types are left unchanged by `_NetworkValueBaseFilter`.

key_constructs.name: NetworkValueRejectlistFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Rejectlist processor for A/AAAA records where IP values must not be inside configured CIDR networks.
key_constructs.reasoning: Inherits `_NetworkValueBaseFilter` and `RejectsMixin`, so A/AAAA with matching network membership are removed; all non-A/AAAA records are left as-is.

key_constructs.name: IgnoreRootNsFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Zone processor that removes root NS records (NS records with empty name).
key_constructs.reasoning: Implements `_process` to remove records when `record._type == 'NS' and not record.name`. Both `process_source_zone` and `process_target_zone` are redirected to this same `_process`, so it always affects both reconciliation phases.

key_constructs.name: ExcludeRootNsChanges
key_constructs.purpose: Plan processor that disallows changes to root NS records by raising ValidationError or removing those changes.
key_constructs.reasoning: Implements `process_plan(plan, sources, target, lenient=False)` and inspects `plan.changes`. It identifies root NS by `record._type == 'NS' and record.name == ''` and either raises `ValidationError(record.fqdn, [...], record.context)` or removes the change from `plan.changes` with a warning.

key_constructs.name: ZoneNameFilter
key_constructs.purpose: Filter/error processor (definition partially truncated in provided snippet) controlling records whose names contain the zone name.
key_constructs.reasoning: Only docstring and header portion are visible in the provided excerpt, but it is imported and exercised in tests. Any edits must preserve behavior expected by `TestZoneNameFilter`.

key_behaviors: Filters/mutates Zone.records by removing records via zone.remove_record(record)
Applies filtering to source zone and optionally to target zone based on include_target
Matches record types via record._type exact set membership
Matches record names via exact matching and regex patterns derived from '/.../' entries
Matches record values using canonical value.rdata_text for single and multi-value records
Logs warning when record value is NoneType and treats it as non-match
Matches A/AAAA record IP values against configured CIDRs (validates CIDRs at init)
Ignores non-A/AAAA records in network value filters
Removes root NS records during zone processing when record.name is empty
Validates plan changes: root NS changes are disallowed (raise or remove depending on error flag)

pitfalls.mistake: Changing how `_ValueBaseFilter` derives `values` from records (e.g., using `str(value)` instead of `rdata_text`).
pitfalls.consequence: Allow/reject matching may become inconsistent with how DNS record data is represented, causing tests and real deployments to filter incorrectly.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve use of `value.rdata_text` for canonical comparisons and keep multi-value handling behavior intact.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering `include_target` semantics so target-zone records are always filtered or never filtered.
pitfalls.consequence: Planned deletions or reconciliation behavior can change, diverging from expected processor configuration in tests (notably `TestTypeAllowListFilter.test_include_target`).
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain `_FilterProcessor.process_target_zone` gating logic.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or changing `None`/lenient behavior in `_ValueBaseFilter` when records have `value=None`.
pitfalls.consequence: Filtering may raise exceptions (AttributeError) or mis-handle records, breaking unit tests that include lenient records.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep defensive handling for missing/None values and ensure behavior remains “ignore” rather than crash.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing regex pattern parsing rules (how patterns starting with '/' are compiled).
pitfalls.consequence: Regex allow/reject filters will interpret pattern strings differently than configuration/tests expect.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve `pattern[1:-1]` stripping and use of `re_compile`.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying how root NS changes are identified in `ExcludeRootNsChanges` (type/name criteria).
pitfalls.consequence: ValidationError may be raised for wrong records or fail to catch disallowed changes, breaking tests and safety expectations.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the identification condition `record._type == 'NS' and record.name == ''` and the error vs remove behavior.

reading_guide.start_here: _FilterProcessor and the allow/reject mixins (AllowsMixin/RejectsMixin), then proceed to each base filter’s _process implementation.
key_sections: _FilterProcessor: include_target gating between source and target processing
AllowsMixin / RejectsMixin: match vs non-match record removal semantics
Type/Name/Value filter base classes: pattern parsing and match evaluation
NetworkValue filters: CIDR validation and A/AAAA-only processing
IgnoreRootNsFilter and ExcludeRootNsChanges: NS root record handling at zone and plan levels
skip_unless_needed: Example usage docstrings (only needed for understanding configuration parameters; not needed for logic comprehension)

test_functions: TestZoneNameFilter (multiple assertions, truncated in provided excerpt)

relevant_snippets.description: Covers TypeAllowlistFilter/TypeRejectlistFilter including include_target behavior
Covers NameAllowlistFilter/NameRejectlistFilter exact and regex matching
Covers ValueAllowlistFilter/ValueRejectlistFilter exact and regex; includes lenient record with value=None
Covers NetworkValueAllowlistFilter/NetworkValueRejectlistFilter including constructor CIDR validation
Covers IgnoreRootNsFilter and ExcludeRootNsChanges plan mutation/exception behavior

related_files.relationship: Unit tests define expected matching/removal semantics for all filters in this module, including edge cases like include_target=False and value=None under lenient records.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any behavioral change in filter matching/removal, NS handling, or plan validation is reflected in these tests.

insights.title: Avoid crash when record has empty/None value in value allow/reject filters
insights.problem: When a record’s `value` is `None`, value filters attempted to access `record.value.rdata_text`, causing an `AttributeError` and breaking filtering.
insights.root_cause: Missing null handling around `record.value.rdata_text` in `_ValueBaseFilter` when records don’t have populated values.
insights.solution: Wrapped value access in `try/except AttributeError` and log a warning; later commits refined the log message to clarify the filter is ignoring the record. Also ensured filter-specific loggers are initialized in `ValueAllowlistFilter`/`ValueRejectlistFilter` constructors.
insights.lesson_learned: Filtering processors should be defensive: treat missing value as non-match rather than an exception, and add explicit tests for `lenient=True` records with `value=None`.

insights.title: Value allow/reject filters generate matches from rdata_text
insights.problem: Value filters previously converted values to strings in a way that could be inconsistent with how record data is represented/serialized (especially for structured values).
insights.root_cause: Using `str(value)` for record values made matching dependent on `__str__`/dict stringification rather than canonical record rdata text form.
insights.solution: Updated _ValueBaseFilter to use `value.rdata_text` (and for single `record.value`, `record.value.rdata_text`) as the basis for allow/reject comparisons.
insights.lesson_learned: When implementing filter semantics for DNS record values, match against canonical wire-format text (rdata_text) rather than object stringification.

insights.title: Refactored filter processors to share matching/removal logic via mixins/base classes
insights.problem: Multiple filter processors (type/name allow/reject) duplicated similar logic for iterating records, deciding match vs non-match, and removing records.
insights.root_cause: Lack of shared abstraction across filter processors led to repeated code paths and inconsistent behavior risks.
insights.solution: Introduced mixins (AllowsMixin/RejectsMixin) and shared base filter classes (_TypeBaseFilter, _NameBaseFilter) to centralize match/non-match handling, and refactored specific processors to inherit common behavior.
insights.lesson_learned: When many processors share the same lifecycle behavior (iterate records -> match -> keep/remove), centralize that logic in a shared base to reduce divergence and regressions.

insights.title: Add include_target flag to filter processors to preserve existing target zone data
insights.problem: Some filter processors always modified the target zone, even when callers wanted the existing target records untouched (which can leave stale/removed filtered records in place depending on plan/apply semantics).
insights.root_cause: Filter processors previously applied the same transformation to both source and target zones, with no option to preserve target data.
insights.solution: Added _FilterProcessor wrapper with include_target (default True). If include_target=False, process_target_zone returns the existing target zone unchanged.
insights.lesson_learned: Consider whether a processor’s behavior should differ between source normalization and target reconciliation; if yes, add explicit flags rather than relying on implicit semantics.

insights.title: Avoid repeated ip_address conversions in network CIDR matching
insights.problem: NetworkValue filters repeatedly called ip_address(value) inside nested generator expressions, recalculating IP parsing for each CIDR check.
insights.root_cause: Inefficient expression structure: recomputed ip objects multiple times via product(record.values, self.networks).
insights.solution: Precomputed ips = [ip_address(value) for value in record.values] and then used product(ips, self.networks) for membership checks.
insights.lesson_learned: When iterating over the cartesian product of values and networks, parse/convert inputs once outside the inner loops to avoid repeated work.

insights.title: Validate CIDR strings at processor construction time for network value filters
insights.problem: Invalid CIDR values provided in processor configuration could cause failures later or lead to unclear runtime errors.
insights.root_cause: CIDR parsing/validation needed to be enforced when building the filter’s internal network list.
insights.solution: In _NetworkValueBaseFilter.__init__, attempted ip_network(value) and raised ValueError(f'{value} is not a valid CIDR to use') on failures.
insights.lesson_learned: Validate configuration early (constructor time) with clear error messages to fail fast and keep runtime processing predictable.

insights.title: ZoneNameFilter default behavior changed to error=True
insights.problem: ZoneNameFilter previously defaulted to error=False (comment indicated default false), but that may have caused invalid/undesired records to be silently ignored.
insights.root_cause: Default configuration value did not match the intended stricter validation behavior.
insights.solution: Changed default parameter error from False to True in ZoneNameFilter.__init__ and updated test usage accordingly.
insights.lesson_learned: Be cautious when changing defaults for validators/safety checks; align tests and documentation with intended operational safety.

octodns/processor/acme.py.short_description: Processor that tags managed ACME TXT challenges and ignores unowned ones on targets.
octodns/processor/acme.py.description:
This file defines an octoDNS processor responsible for ACME DNS-01 challenge TXT records. It participates in the provider/zone processing pipeline by implementing two hooks: `process_source_zone` (runs when building the desired state) and `process_target_zone` (runs when comparing against the existing state). Its primary purpose is to ensure octoDNS can claim ownership of specific ACME TXT challenge records by tagging them with a sentinel value (`*octoDNS*`) and then to prevent octoDNS from deleting other ACME challenge TXT records it does not manage.

In `process_source_zone`, the processor scans the desired zone's records for TXT records whose `name` starts with `_acme-challenge`. For each such record, it copies the record, appends `*octoDNS*` to the record's values, sorts the values, records the modified record into an internal `_owned` set, and then re-adds it into `desired` with `replace=True`. This assumes a particular ordering in processing (“we'll see things as sources before targets”), which is necessary for `_owned` to be populated before `process_target_zone` runs.

In `process_target_zone`, the processor iterates the existing zone’s records and removes TXT challenge records that match `_acme-challenge*` but do not contain `*octoDNS*` and were not recorded in `_owned`. It intentionally uses `startswith('_acme-challenge')` rather than exact match to avoid affecting subdomain ACME challenges (e.g., when managing `domain.com`), while still broadly targeting the ACME TXT namespace. The class also provides a backwards/alternate symbol alias `AcmeMangingProcessor` that references `AcmeManagingProcessor` (note the misspelling), which is likely used by older configuration or documentation.

Because this processor mutates records by adding a sentinel value and then relies on record membership in `_owned`, correctness depends on how records are compared for equality and hashing within octoDNS. Any edits here must preserve the sentinel marker semantics, the ownership tracking, and the call ordering assumptions to avoid inadvertently deleting active ACME challenge records.

key_constructs.name: AcmeManagingProcessor
key_constructs.purpose: Implements source/target zone processing for managed ACME TXT challenge records.
key_constructs.reasoning: The processor’s lifecycle hooks (`process_source_zone` and `process_target_zone`) define how octoDNS claims ownership and prunes unowned ACME challenge TXT records.

key_constructs.name: process_source_zone
key_constructs.purpose: Tags desired ACME TXT records with `*octoDNS*` and tracks them as owned.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ownership tracking via `_owned` is required later when filtering existing target records; the sentinel addition is the mechanism used to recognize managed records.

key_constructs.name: process_target_zone
key_constructs.purpose: Removes existing unowned ACME TXT records that do not include the sentinel.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is where deletion avoidance/cleanup happens; it must match the same record naming and sentinel semantics used in `process_source_zone`.

key_constructs.name: AcmeMangingProcessor
key_constructs.purpose: Alias pointing to `AcmeManagingProcessor` (spelled incorrectly).
key_constructs.reasoning: Configuration compatibility: code elsewhere may reference the misspelled name; removing or renaming it could break existing configs.

key_behaviors: Tags desired ACME challenge TXT record values by appending `*octoDNS*`
Tracks tagged records in an internal `_owned` set for later reconciliation
Deletes existing ACME challenge TXT records that lack the sentinel and are not in `_owned`

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the sentinel marker string (`*octoDNS*`) or how it is added to `record.values`
pitfalls.consequence: The target-side filter may fail to recognize managed records, causing octoDNS to delete live ACME challenge TXT records or to fail to clean up.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the exact sentinel string and ensure it is included in the record values before `_owned` tracking and target filtering.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering the ordering assumption that source processing occurs before target processing
pitfalls.consequence: If `_owned` is not populated before `process_target_zone` runs, the processor may treat managed records as unowned and remove them.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure any changes do not break or bypass the lifecycle/order that populates `_owned` before target reconciliation.

pitfalls.mistake: Replacing `record.name.startswith('_acme-challenge')` logic with equality-only matching or changing the match scope
pitfalls.consequence: ACME TXT records on subdomains (or varying FQDN patterns) may be incorrectly deleted or ignored.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the naming match semantics consistent with the intent to target the ACME challenge namespace.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or renaming the alias `AcmeMangingProcessor`
pitfalls.consequence: Existing configurations referencing the misspelled class name may stop working.
pitfalls.prevention: Retain the alias or provide compatibility for any existing config references.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying how `_owned` membership is checked without understanding record equality/hash semantics
pitfalls.consequence: If `DNSRecord` objects do not hash/equal as expected, the `record not in self._owned` check may not behave correctly, leading to unintended removals.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the ownership tracking mechanism and avoid changing the record object identity/equality assumptions unless you confirm `DNSRecord` equality/hash behavior.

key_sections: process_source_zone: how desired TXT records are copied, tagged, sorted, and recorded in `_owned`
process_target_zone: how existing TXT records are filtered using sentinel presence and `_owned` membership
skip_unless_needed: Docstring example configuration: only relevant for understanding expected processor wiring and zone configuration

related_files.relationship: Defines `BaseProcessor` and likely the `lenient` behavior and lifecycle contract for `process_source_zone` / `process_target_zone` hooks.
related_files.reason_to_check: To understand how `lenient` is propagated and how processors are invoked in source vs target passes.

related_files.relationship: Defines the `Zone` object and its `records` iteration, and methods like `add_record` / `remove_record` used here.
related_files.reason_to_check: To confirm record comparison, copying behavior, and whether `remove_record` relies on identity or equality.

related_files.relationship: May export/resolve processor class names used by configuration.
related_files.reason_to_check: To see how `AcmeMangingProcessor` (misspelling) might be referenced/registered.

octodns/idna.py.short_description: Utility helpers for IDNA encode/decode and a case/IDNA-insensitive mapping.
octodns/idna.py.description:
This module provides thin wrappers around the third-party `idna` library to support IDNA handling in the places where providers send/receive DNS names. The intent is that providers can normalize domain labels consistently right before making API calls (encode) and later interpret API responses (decode).

The file defines a custom `IdnaError` exception to wrap `idna.IDNAError`, preserving the error message while presenting a stable octoDNS-level exception type to callers. It then implements label-by-label encoding/decoding (via `idna_encode`/`idna_decode`) to support names that may contain a mixture of ASCII and non-ASCII labels, and to allow certain ASCII characters that IDNA rules may treat differently at the whole-name level.

Finally, it defines `IdnaDict`, a `MutableMapping` whose keys are stored using `idna_encode(k)`. This makes the mapping effectively insensitive to case and to whether keys are supplied in ASCII form or as already-IDNA encoded labels. It also provides `decoded_keys()` and `decoded_items()` helpers so callers can iterate over the original/unencoded representation of keys when presenting or comparing results.

key_constructs.name: IdnaError
key_constructs.purpose: Exception wrapper used to re-raise IDNA failures in a module-specific type.
key_constructs.reasoning: Callers can catch `IdnaError` rather than the third-party `idna.IDNAError`, providing a consistent error type across the codebase.

callers.context: from octodns.idna import IdnaDict, IdnaError, idna_decode, idna_encode

key_constructs.name: encode
key_constructs.purpose: Encode a string to IDNA if it contains non-ASCII characters.
key_constructs.reasoning: Avoids unnecessary work for pure-ASCII inputs, while still converting non-ASCII characters into their IDNA/punycode representation.

key_constructs.name: idna_encode
key_constructs.purpose: IDNA-encode a full DNS name by processing each dot-separated label in lowercase.
key_constructs.reasoning: Supports mixed-IDNA/ASCII names and ensures consistent normalization (lowercasing) for keying and API calls.

callers.context: self.name = idna_encode(str(name)) if name else name

key_constructs.name: decode
key_constructs.purpose: Decode a single label if it appears to be punycode (starts with 'xn--').
key_constructs.reasoning: Prevents attempting to decode labels that are not IDNA-encoded.

key_constructs.name: idna_decode
key_constructs.purpose: Decode a full DNS name by applying `decode` label-by-label after lowercasing.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures consistent interpretation across mixed labels and normalizes case before decoding.

callers.context: msg = f'Invalid alias zone {decoded_zone_name}: source zone {idna_decode(source_zone)} does not exis
msg = f'Invalid alias zone {decoded_zone_name}: source zone {idna_decode(source_zone)} is an alias z
f'Invalid zone name {idna_decode(zone_name)}, missing ending dot'
f'invalid fqdn, "{idna_decode(fqdn)}" is too long at {n} '

key_constructs.name: IdnaDict
key_constructs.purpose: A MutableMapping whose keys are normalized using `idna_encode` for storage and lookup.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides case- and IDNA-insensitive dictionary semantics for DNS-name-keyed data structures.

callers.context: zones = IdnaDict({n: zones.get(n) for n in eligible_zones})
from octodns.idna import IdnaDict, IdnaError, idna_decode, idna_encode

key_behaviors: encodes each label of a domain name independently using IDNA rules
normalizes domain name input by lowercasing before encoding/decoding
decodes labels only when they appear punycode via 'xn--' prefix
stores dictionary keys as IDNA-encoded forms so lookups ignore case and punycode vs Unicode

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming `decode()` always transforms input.
pitfalls.consequence: Non-'xn--' labels (or already-unencoded ASCII) will be returned unchanged, which may surprise callers expecting a full decode pass.
pitfalls.prevention: Use `idna_decode()` for full-name behavior or understand that `decode()` is conditional on the 'xn--' prefix.

pitfalls.mistake: Bypassing `idna_encode()` and storing keys without normalization when using `IdnaDict`-like semantics elsewhere.
pitfalls.consequence: Lookups may fail or duplicate entries may appear because `IdnaDict` expects keys to be normalized to its internal punycode/lowercase representation.
pitfalls.prevention: Route all key creation/lookup through `IdnaDict` (or mimic its normalization rules exactly).

pitfalls.mistake: Feeding strings with empty labels or unusual dot structures to the label-splitting logic without accounting for behavior.
pitfalls.consequence: Because `name.lower().split('.')` processes each element, empty labels (e.g., leading/trailing dots or consecutive dots) will be passed to `encode()` and may trigger IDNA errors.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure upstream normalization/validation produces conventional dot-separated domain labels before invoking these helpers.

pitfalls.mistake: Catching `idna.IDNAError` directly instead of `IdnaError` when using these helpers.
pitfalls.consequence: IDNA failures are re-raised as `IdnaError`, so direct catches may miss errors and propagate unexpectedly.
pitfalls.prevention: Catch `IdnaError` at the call sites that use `idna_encode`/`idna_decode`/`encode` paths.

key_sections: encode: conditional ASCII fast-path and conversion of non-ascii to IDNA via third-party encode()
idna_encode: per-label processing (lowercase + label split + join) and wrapping of IDNA errors
decode/idna_decode: conditional 'xn--' decoding and per-label join behavior
IdnaDict: how it normalizes keys in __setitem__/__getitem__/__delitem__ and how decoded_keys/decoded_items reconstruct presentation

octodns/secret/environ.py.short_description: Fetches secret values from environment variables with optional VAR/DEFAULT parsing.
octodns/secret/environ.py.description:
This module implements an environment-backed secrets source for octodns. It defines a dedicated exception type and a secrets handler that resolves a configured “secret name” into a value by looking up a process environment variable. The handler supports an inline default syntax where the configured name can include a slash-delimited fallback.

The central logic lives in EnvironSecrets.fetch(name, source). It first parses the incoming name for a default by splitting on the first '/'. It then attempts to read environ[name] (with the possibly modified name after the split). If the environment variable is missing, it returns the parsed default value when provided; otherwise it logs and raises EnvironSecretsException with context derived from source.context.

After determining the raw string value (from env or default), the handler performs a simple numeric conversion heuristic: if the value contains a '.', it tries float conversion; otherwise it tries int conversion. If the conversion raises ValueError, the value remains a string. This behavior is exercised directly by unit tests, including defaults containing slashes, empty defaults, and precedence rules (env var value overrides default).

key_constructs.name: EnvironSecretsException
key_constructs.purpose: Specialized exception for errors raised by EnvironSecrets when env-backed config is invalid.
key_constructs.reasoning: The Manager and unit tests expect this specific exception type for missing env vars; keep its inheritance/usage consistent to preserve exception matching.

key_constructs.name: EnvironSecrets
key_constructs.purpose: Secrets handler that resolves secret values from environment variables, with optional inline defaults.
key_constructs.reasoning: Acts as the concrete implementation of BaseSecrets for the 'env' secrets backend; its fetch contract determines how provider configs interpret missing secrets.

key_constructs.name: fetch
key_constructs.purpose: Resolve a configured secret name to a value from environ, optionally using a default value and attempting int/float conversion.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the behavioral boundary that tests cover: default parsing on '/', precedence, type coercion rules, and exception message contents.

key_behaviors: parses default syntax from configured name using VARIABLE/DEFAULT
returns env var value when present and ignores any default
returns DEFAULT_VALUE when env var is missing and default is provided
raises EnvironSecretsException when env var is missing and default is not provided
attempts to convert resolved values to float (if '.' in string) or int (otherwise), falling back to string

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the default parsing logic from splitting on the first '/' to something else
pitfalls.consequence: Defaults containing slashes (e.g., URLs) would be incorrectly truncated, breaking expected behavior and tests.
