pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the 'split('/', 1)' semantics so only the first slash is treated as the delimiter.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering the exception message format or removing source.context from it
pitfalls.consequence: Unit tests and Manager-level assertions that check for substrings or exact message content may fail.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the raised EnvironSecretsException message consistent with expectations.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying the numeric coercion heuristic ('.' => float, else => int)
pitfalls.consequence: Type expectations for integer/float env values and defaults would change and tests would fail.
pitfalls.prevention: Retain the current conversion branching and ValueError fallback-to-string behavior.

pitfalls.mistake: Trying to convert empty default values or changing how empty strings behave
pitfalls.consequence: Empty defaults are expected to remain empty strings; conversion changes could raise errors or change types.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure the int/float conversion failures continue to be caught and return the original string.

key_sections: name parsing for default values (the '/' handling and split('/', 1))
env lookup and default/exception decision block
numeric conversion heuristic and ValueError fallback
skip_unless_needed: EnvironSecretsException (simple subclass; only relevant for error matching)

relevant_snippets.description: Covers env var lookup, int/float conversion, missing env var exception message, and default parsing/precedence including slashes and empty defaults.

related_files.relationship: Direct unit tests validating EnvironSecrets.fetch behavior including type coercion and exception messaging.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any behavioral change in fetch will require updating these assertions and possibly how exceptions are raised.

related_files.relationship: Manager integration test asserts that missing env config bubbles up as EnvironSecretsException.
related_files.reason_to_check: If exception types or message semantics change, Manager tests expecting them will break.

insights.title: Support default values for env-backed secrets using VAR_NAME/DEFAULT
insights.problem: Environment-backed secret values (env/AWS_* etc.) had no mechanism to specify a fallback default when an env var is missing.
insights.root_cause: EnvironSecrets.fetch only expanded `environ[name]` and raised when the key was absent, with no parsing of defaults in the configured name string.
insights.solution: Parse `name` as `VARIABLE/DEFAULT_VALUE` (splitting on the first `/`) and return DEFAULT_VALUE when the env var is missing. Existing env values take precedence over defaults.
insights.lesson_learned: When adding configuration flexibility, ensure parsing rules cover boundary cases (defaults containing `/`, empty defaults) and add integration tests that prove precedence (default ignored if env var exists).

insights.title: Consistent naming for secret exceptions and EnvironSecrets
insights.problem: Exception class naming was inconsistent (singular vs plural), which propagated into Manager and test expectations.
insights.root_cause: Initial secret handler implementation introduced SecretException/EnvironSecretException naming that later diverged from intended pluralized conventions for handlers.
insights.solution: Renamed SecretException to SecretsException, EnvironSecretException to EnvironSecretsException, and updated raise/import sites accordingly.
insights.lesson_learned: Adopt consistent naming early (especially for public-ish exception types); after introducing new abstractions (like secret handlers), keep naming stable to avoid cascading test/API churn.

tests/test_octodns_secret_environ.py.short_description: Unit tests for EnvironSecrets.fetch: env lookup, default parsing, coercion, and errors.
tests/test_octodns_secret_environ.py.description:
This test module verifies the behavior of octodns.secret.environ.EnvironSecrets. It is a focused unit test that sets up environment variables using os.environ, instantiates EnvironSecrets('env'), and calls fetch() with a ContextDict providing context='xyz'.

The test covers multiple branches: successful retrieval with string, integer, and float env values; raising EnvironSecretsException when an env var is missing and no default is specified; and the inline default syntax name/DEFAULT_VALUE including int/float conversion for numeric defaults, defaults containing additional slashes (split on first slash only), precedence where the existing env var overrides an ignored default, and the behavior of an empty default (name ends with '/', resulting in '' returned as a string).

Because the exception message is asserted exactly (including the env var name and the source context), any change to the fetch exception formatting or how source.context is used will break this test.

key_constructs.name: TestEnvironSecrets
key_constructs.purpose: TestCase grouping for EnvironSecrets-specific unit tests.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a single comprehensive test method that covers all relevant fetch paths.

key_constructs.name: test_environ_secrets
key_constructs.purpose: Exercizes EnvironSecrets.fetch across success, default usage, numeric coercion, precedence, and missing-variable error handling.
key_constructs.reasoning: Acts as the specification for the handler’s parsing and type conversion rules.

key_behaviors: modifies process environment via environ[...] assignments for test inputs
verifies returned types for env-backed numeric values
verifies exact exception message when env var is missing without a default
verifies default value parsing including defaults with slashes and empty defaults

pitfalls.mistake: Relying on environment variables outside those set in the test without cleanup/isolated configuration
pitfalls.consequence: Tests could become flaky if the same env var names exist in the test runner environment.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep assertions tied to known keys and values; if changing the handler to reference other env keys, ensure the test sets/controls them accordingly.

key_sections: setup: environ assignments for THIS_EXISTS/THIS_IS_AN_INT/THIS_IS_A_FLOAT
success cases and type assertions
missing env var exception case with exact message check
default parsing cases: numeric defaults, slashes in defaults, precedence, empty default
skip_unless_needed: imports and class wrapper

relevant_snippets.description: The entire test body documents the expected semantics for parsing defaults, coercion, and exception message contents.

related_files.relationship: Directly tests EnvironSecrets.fetch behavior and EnvironSecretsException message.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any update to fetch logic must maintain the semantics asserted here.

related_files.relationship: Ensures Manager propagates EnvironSecretsException when provider env config is missing.
related_files.reason_to_check: If exception type/message changes, adjust Manager test expectations if they become too strict.

octodns/processor/base.py.short_description: Defines BaseProcessor and ProcessorException for zone/plan processing hooks.
octodns/processor/base.py.description:
This module provides the core abstractions for octoDNS “processors”: pluggable objects that run at multiple points in the sync lifecycle to transform or validate zone and plan data. It defines a common base class, `BaseProcessor`, that downstream processor implementations inherit from, and a dedicated exception type, `ProcessorException`, which processors can raise to signal processing-specific failures.

`BaseProcessor` is intentionally minimal: it implements the public hook methods (`process_source_zone`, `process_target_zone`, `process_source_and_target_zones`, and `process_plan`) with default pass-through behavior. The rest of octoDNS is expected to instantiate configured processor classes, call these hooks in sequence, and honor their returned values. The extensive docstrings act as a contract for processors: they clarify what “desired” and “existing” represent at each hook phase, the ordering relative to provider processing, and rules around modifying records (e.g., copy records rather than mutate directly).

A notable behavioral detail is the handling of “lenient” mode. `BaseProcessor.__init__` stores an instance-level `lenient`, and each hook accepts a per-call `lenient` flag; processor implementations are expected to combine them (documented as `self.lenient or lenient`) when calling into zone/record mutation APIs that accept `lenient`. The base class itself simply returns inputs; the lenient combination responsibility is on subclasses and is described to enforce consistency across processor implementations.

key_constructs.name: ProcessorException
key_constructs.purpose: Exception type processors can raise when processing fails.
key_constructs.reasoning: Having a dedicated exception allows the rest of the pipeline/CLI to distinguish processor errors from other errors and handle them consistently.

key_constructs.name: BaseProcessor
key_constructs.purpose: Base class defining the processor lifecycle hook methods and instance configuration.
key_constructs.reasoning: Downstream processors rely on the stable method names and return contracts documented here so the orchestrator can safely compose multiple processors in order.

key_constructs.name: process_source_zone
key_constructs.purpose: Hook to modify the desired zone after sources populate and before planning.
key_constructs.reasoning: This hook is the entry point for processors that need to change the target state used for planning.

key_constructs.name: process_target_zone
key_constructs.purpose: Hook to modify the existing zone after target populates and before planning.
key_constructs.reasoning: This hook enables processors that normalize, filter, or validate what a target provider reports as existing.

key_constructs.name: process_source_and_target_zones
key_constructs.purpose: Hook to modify both desired and existing zones just before computing changes for a specific target.
key_constructs.reasoning: Some transformations require comparing or coordinating edits across both states before the plan is generated.

key_constructs.name: process_plan
key_constructs.purpose: Hook to modify the computed plan after planning and before applying.
key_constructs.reasoning: This allows processors to adjust the action set (changes) presented/applied, potentially even when the plan was produced as None or contains no changes.

key_behaviors: Defines default processor hook methods that return inputs unchanged
Exposes multiple phase-specific processing hooks for desired/existing/plan
Declares lenient-mode semantics expected to be combined as self.lenient or lenient
Provides a dedicated exception type for processors to signal processing errors

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying zone records directly inside processor hooks without using record.copy and Zone.add_record/replace semantics (as described in the docstrings).
pitfalls.consequence: The pipeline may end up with inconsistent zone state, incorrect diffing/plan computation, or unintended mutation effects across processors.
pitfalls.prevention: Follow the documented modification contract: copy records, modify the copy, and re-add with appropriate replace behavior; use remove_record only when intended.

pitfalls.mistake: Forgetting to pass combined lenient mode into any zone/record APIs that accept a lenient parameter.
pitfalls.consequence: Validation behavior becomes inconsistent with other processors and the intended relaxed-mode semantics, potentially causing unexpected exceptions or overly strict validation.
pitfalls.prevention: In subclasses, combine `self.lenient or lenient` and propagate that to zone/record operations that accept lenient.

pitfalls.mistake: Ignoring the return-contract of each hook (returning None or returning the wrong object).
pitfalls.consequence: The orchestrator may lose intended modifications or fail when it expects the returned desired/existing/plan to continue through subsequent phases.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure each overridden hook returns the modified zone/plan objects as specified by the base docstrings.

pitfalls.mistake: Depending on `name` semantics instead of `id` (despite this base class storing both).
pitfalls.consequence: Processor identity handling may change in future versions (docstring indicates `name` is deprecated and removal is planned), leading to mismatches in logging/config references.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat `id` as the stable identifier expected by the broader system; be cautious when introducing new dependencies on `name`.

key_sections: ProcessorException: dedicated error type for processors
BaseProcessor.__init__: assigns id/name and stores instance-level lenient flag
process_source_zone / process_target_zone / process_source_and_target_zones / process_plan: hook contracts and default pass-through returns
skip_unless_needed: Large docstring examples that show YAML configuration unless you are adjusting processor docs or config schema.

related_files.relationship: Concrete processor implementations inherit from BaseProcessor and rely on these hook method names and lenient/return contracts.
related_files.reason_to_check: When modifying hook signatures or documented contracts, processor implementations and their behavior across phases must remain consistent.

related_files.relationship: The sync/plan/apply orchestrator is responsible for invoking processor hooks in the correct order with the right zone/plan arguments.
related_files.reason_to_check: If hook behavior or return expectations change, the orchestration logic must match.

related_files.relationship: Providers populate zones and may include logic that occurs before/after processor hooks; the processor base docstrings reference provider processing steps.
related_files.reason_to_check: Lenient mode and zone/record modification semantics must align with provider-produced Zone objects and their mutability expectations.

octodns/record/naptr.py.short_description: Implements NAPTR record value parsing/validation/rendering and templating.
octodns/record/naptr.py.description:
This module defines the NAPTR-specific value model (`NaptrValue`) and record wrapper (`NaptrRecord`). `NaptrValue` is a dictionary-like object (subclassing `dict`) with a structured schema: `order`, `preference`, `flags`, `service`, `regexp`, and `replacement`. It integrates with the project’s equality and record framework via `EqualityTupleMixin`, `ValuesMixin`, and `Record`. The NAPTR record type is registered at the end with `Record.register_type(NaptrRecord)` so that generic record creation/dispatch logic can instantiate `NaptrRecord` from type strings like `NAPTR`.

Key responsibilities inside `NaptrValue` include: parsing NAPTR RDATA text (`parse_rdata_text`) from RR text into component fields; validating a list of value dicts (`validate`) and returning a list of human-readable reasons (rather than throwing directly); normalizing/constructing the internal representation in `__init__` by coercing `order`/`preference` to integers and uppercasing `flags`; rendering back to RR text (`rdata_text`) according to RFC3403’s quoting requirements for `flags`, `service`, and `regexp`; and applying string formatting templates (`template`) across multiple subfields (`service`, `regexp`, `replacement`) when placeholders are present. Equality and hashing are driven by `_equality_tuple()` and `__repr__()` (via `__hash__`).

This code is exercised heavily by `tests/test_octodns_record_naptr.py`, which asserts parsing edge cases, validation error reason strings, flags case-insensitivity/normalization, templating behavior, and ordering/comparison semantics (through `NaptrValue`’s mixin-based ordering/equality). Any changes to parsing, validation, normalization, or the string rendering format must remain consistent with those tests and with how the broader record framework expects value objects to behave.

key_constructs.name: NaptrValue
key_constructs.purpose: Dictionary-backed NAPTR value object with parsing, validation, rendering, equality, and templating.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the core type used by `NaptrRecord`. Its parsing/validation/rendering/templating behavior directly determines how RR text and provider data get normalized and compared for diffs.

key_constructs.name: parse_rdata_text
key_constructs.purpose: Parse NAPTR RDATA text into a dict of fields, unquoting relevant parts and int-coercing order/preference when possible.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used when converting provider RR text into internal value dicts/objects. The number of whitespace-delimited tokens is a strict parsing constraint.

key_constructs.name: validate
key_constructs.purpose: Validate a list of candidate value dicts and return a list of error reason strings.
key_constructs.reasoning: The record framework expects `validate` to be non-throwing and to return reasons in specific wording, which the unit tests assert.

key_constructs.name: process
key_constructs.purpose: Convert raw value dicts into `NaptrValue` instances.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the construction hook for `NaptrRecord`/`ValuesMixin` pipelines.

key_constructs.name: rdata_text
key_constructs.purpose: Render the NAPTR value as RR text, quoting flags/service/regexp per RFC3403 requirements.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used when providers or internal logic emit RR text; tests assert exact formatting including quoting placement and replacement unquoted.

key_constructs.name: template
key_constructs.purpose: Apply `.format(**params)` templating to `service`, `regexp`, and `replacement` when placeholders are present.
key_constructs.reasoning: Supports dynamic record/value expansion for providers that use formatting placeholders; tests verify both noop and multi-field formatting.

key_constructs.name: __init__
key_constructs.purpose: Normalize an input mapping into internal canonical types/values (int order/preference, uppercase flags).
key_constructs.reasoning: This normalization affects equality/hashing and diff stability across providers with differing capitalization.

key_constructs.name: _equality_tuple
key_constructs.purpose: Define the tuple used for equality/ordering comparisons across `NaptrValue` objects.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ordering comparisons in tests rely on a deterministic field ordering (order, preference, flags, service, regexp, replacement).

key_constructs.name: NaptrRecord
key_constructs.purpose: Record wrapper for NAPTR, binding `_value_type = NaptrValue` and `_type = 'NAPTR'`.
key_constructs.reasoning: This lets the generic record framework create NAPTR records and perform value list diffs/serialization via the `ValuesMixin` behavior.

key_behaviors: Parses NAPTR RDATA text by splitting into exactly six space-delimited tokens (with optional quoting handled via `unquote`).
Coerces `order` and `preference` to integers in the canonical stored representation (and preserves original non-int text only long enough to validate/parse).
Uppercases flags during `NaptrValue` construction and renders flags as quoted strings in `rdata_text`.
Validates presence and type-castability of `order`/`preference`, validates flags against `VALID_FLAGS`, and checks presence of `service`/`regexp`/`replacement` keys.
Renders flags/service/regexp as quoted character-strings while leaving `replacement` unquoted in `rdata_text`.
Applies template substitution only when placeholders (`'{'`) appear in any of `service`, `regexp`, or `replacement`.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the strict tokenization logic in `parse_rdata_text` (it expects exactly six space-separated segments).
pitfalls.consequence: RR text parsing will start accepting/rejecting different provider strings, breaking test expectations and potentially causing provider data import failures.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the exact parsing contract: raising `RrParseError()` on ValueError from unpacking due to wrong token counts.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering validation reason wording or the set/order of returned reasons.
pitfalls.consequence: Unit tests assert exact `ctx.exception.reasons` strings; diffs and CLI/reporting may also depend on these reason messages.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep `validate` returning the same strings for missing/invalid fields and understand tests cover these messages.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing or changing canonicalization of `flags` (uppercasing) in `__init__`.
pitfalls.consequence: Equality/diff stability may change when providers vary only in flags capitalization; tests explicitly check case normalization behavior.
pitfalls.prevention: Retain canonical storage of flags via `.upper()` and keep `VALID_FLAGS` consistent with expected accepted values.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying `rdata_text` quoting rules.
pitfalls.consequence: Generated RR text may diverge from RFC3403 expectations and from unit tests that assert exact formatting with quotes around `flags`, `service`, and `regexp` only.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the formatting string structure used in `rdata_text`.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing which fields templating applies to or the placeholder detection logic.
pitfalls.consequence: Templating may become partial or incorrectly applied; tests validate noop behavior when no placeholders exist and that all template-capable subfields are formatted.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the `'{' in ...` short-circuit and the use of `.format(**params)` on `service`, `regexp`, and `replacement`.

key_sections: NaptrValue.parse_rdata_text: strict 6-token split + unquote + optional int coercion
NaptrValue.validate: reasons list generation (missing/invalid order, preference, flags, and missing keys)
NaptrValue.__init__ and flags normalization: canonical int coercion + `flags.upper()`
NaptrValue.rdata_text: RR text rendering including RFC3403 quoting
NaptrValue.template: placeholder detection and formatting across multiple subfields
NaptrValue._equality_tuple: field order used for equality/ordering comparisons
skip_unless_needed: __repr__ and __hash__: mostly for safe representation and set/dict behavior; tests only ensure it doesn’t blow up and that hashing/equality work.

relevant_snippets.description: Covers NaptrValue/NaptrRecord construction, changes/diff behavior, ordering comparisons, parsing errors, validation reasons, and flags normalization.

related_files.relationship: Unit tests assert exact parsing, validation reason strings, flag case handling, ordering/equality behavior, and rdata_text formatting for NaptrValue/NaptrRecord.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any behavioral change in NaptrValue (parsing/validation/rendering/equality/templating) must remain consistent with these assertions.

insights.title: NAPTR flags are case insensitive and output normalized to uppercase
insights.problem: NAPTR flags parsing was not fully case-insensitive, potentially rejecting valid inputs or producing unwanted persistent changes when providers vary capitalization.
insights.root_cause: VALID_FLAGS did not include lowercase and stored flags without normalization.
insights.solution: Expanded `VALID_FLAGS` to include lowercase variants and normalized parsing with `value['flags'].upper()` so validation accepts both cases but stored flags always become uppercase.
insights.lesson_learned: Where RFCs specify case-insensitive fields, validate case-insensitively and normalize outputs to a canonical form to prevent perpetual diffs.

insights.title: Verify templating for multiple NAPTR fields
insights.problem: NAPTR record value fields (`service`, `regexp`, `replacement`) may contain placeholders and must be expanded correctly.
insights.root_cause: Templating needed consistent behavior across multiple subfields.
insights.solution: Added tests verifying noop when no placeholders exist and correct formatting for each placeholder-bearing field.
insights.lesson_learned: When a value object has multiple templatable fields, ensure tests validate each field individually to avoid partial/incorrect formatting.

tests/test_octodns_record_naptr.py.short_description: Unit tests for NAPTR record/value parsing, validation, equality, ordering, and templating.
tests/test_octodns_record_naptr.py.description:
This file provides the unit test coverage for the NAPTR record implementation. It uses the project’s `Zone`, `Record`, and record exception types to verify that `NaptrRecord` and `NaptrValue` integrate correctly with the generic record framework: record creation via `NaptrRecord(...)` and `Record.new(...)`, serialization via `rdata_text`, comparison/diff logic via `changes`, and validation via raising `ValidationError` with specific reason strings.

The tests are organized into two classes: `TestRecordNaptr` focuses on end-to-end record/value behaviors, including parsing RR text edge cases, strict validation expectations, canonicalization of flags across input case variants, and ordering comparisons (greater-than/less-than) that depend on `NaptrValue`’s mixin-derived comparison semantics. `TestNaptrValue` (partially shown) focuses on templating behavior for `NaptrValue.template`, which must apply `.format(**params)` to multiple fields and leave values unchanged when no placeholders exist. This suite acts as the behavioral contract for any modifications to `octodns/record/naptr.py`.

key_constructs.name: TestRecordNaptr
key_constructs.purpose: Tests NAPTR record/value creation, parsing/rendering, validation errors, changes/diffing, and ordering/equality semantics.
key_constructs.reasoning: Validates how `NaptrValue` and `NaptrRecord` behave inside the record framework and against expected RFC formatting.

key_constructs.name: test_naptr
key_constructs.purpose: Checks NaptrRecord fields mapping, `data` round-trip, and `changes` detection for specific value differences.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures diff stability by verifying that order and replacement fields cause changes as expected while identical values do not.

key_constructs.name: test_naptr_value_rdata_text
key_constructs.purpose: Covers strict parse error handling (`RrParseError`), parsing int coercion behavior, unquoting behavior, and exact `rdata_text` rendering.
key_constructs.reasoning: Defines the serialization/parsing contract used across provider imports and exports.

key_constructs.name: test_validation
key_constructs.purpose: Asserts `Record.new` raises `ValidationError` with precise reason strings for missing keys and invalid types/flags.
key_constructs.reasoning: Locks down the exact error messages returned by `NaptrValue.validate`.

key_constructs.name: test_flags_case_insensitive
key_constructs.purpose: Ensures flags are accepted in uppercase and lowercase and normalized to uppercase; mixed/invalid flags must fail.
key_constructs.reasoning: Captures canonicalization requirements that affect diff stability across providers.

key_behaviors: Verifies `NaptrRecord` exposes `name`, `fqdn`, and `ttl` derived from inputs.
Verifies record value field mapping by comparing each key in value dicts to corresponding `NaptrValue` attributes.
Verifies `NaptrRecord.data` matches the original input structure keys (`values` vs `value`).
Verifies `changes()` detects differences when `order` or `replacement` differs, and returns false when comparing to itself.
Verifies NaptrValue comparison operators (==, !=, <=, >=, <, >) are consistent and ordered by the equality tuple fields.
Verifies parse errors are raised when the RR text has the wrong number of whitespace-separated tokens.
Verifies unquoting behavior when parsing quoted string fields and exact quoting behavior in `rdata_text` rendering.
Verifies `Record.new` validation raises `ValidationError` with exact reason strings for missing fields, invalid numeric fields, and invalid flags.
Verifies accepted flags are case-insensitive at input and normalized to uppercase in stored values.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the exact strings of validation reasons expected by `test_validation` or the exact quoting format expected by `test_naptr_value_rdata_text`.
pitfalls.consequence: Tests will fail even if behavior is functionally similar, because they assert exact list equality of `ctx.exception.reasons` and exact `rdata_text` strings.
pitfalls.prevention: When editing NAPTR parsing/validation/rendering, re-check these test assertions for exact expected strings and formatting.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering comparison/equality semantics by changing `_equality_tuple` field order.
pitfalls.consequence: Ordering-related assertions in `test_naptr` (multiple >/< comparisons) will fail.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat the tuple field order in `NaptrValue` as part of the test contract.

key_sections: TestRecordNaptr.test_naptr: diffing and comparison operator expectations
TestRecordNaptr.test_naptr_value_rdata_text: parse failure cases + exact RR text formatting
TestRecordNaptr.test_validation: exact `ValidationError.reasons` assertions
TestRecordNaptr.test_flags_case_insensitive: accepted flags and canonicalization behavior
TestNaptrValue.test_template: templating contract for service/regexp/replacement (see truncated remainder in provided snippet)
skip_unless_needed: The individual property-set assertions at the end of `test_naptr` are primarily to ensure attribute setters/getters are wired correctly and don’t raise.

relevant_snippets.description: Main NAPTR record tests: construction, `changes()` behavior, and value comparisons ordering by tuple fields.
Parsing and RR text rendering contract: strict token count, unquoting, and exact `rdata_text` output.
Validation contracts: missing keys, invalid ints, and unrecognized flags with exact reason strings.

related_files.relationship: Direct target of these tests; tests codify the behavioral contract for parsing, validation, rendering, equality/order, and templating.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any modifications to `NaptrValue`/`NaptrRecord` should be verified against these assertions.

octodns/processor/trailing_dots.py.short_description: Processor that ensures DNS record targets/values end with '.' where required.
octodns/processor/trailing_dots.py.description:
This module defines a `BaseProcessor` implementation, `EnsureTrailingDots`, whose job is to normalize certain DNS record fields to ensure they end with a trailing dot. The normalization is applied during `process_source_zone`, which is part of octoDNS’s pipeline for transforming a desired zone based on configured processors and source/destination behavior.

The processor handles record types that commonly represent domain names in DNS RR data: `ALIAS`, `CNAME`, and `DNAME` (single value stored on `record.value`), `NS` and `PTR` (multiple values stored on `record.values`), and `MX`/`SRV` (structured data where the relevant string field is `exchange` for `MX` and `target` for `SRV`). For each of these, it conditionally appends a '.' only if it’s missing.

A key design constraint is preserving the value object type (class) used by octoDNS RR models. The code constructs new value instances via `.__class__(f'{...}.')` rather than concatenating into plain strings. This preserves subclass-specific behavior that downstream code/tests may rely on. The updated record is added back to `desired` using `desired.add_record(..., replace=True, lenient=lenient)`, so the transformed record overwrites the original record for the zone planning process. The method returns the mutated `desired` zone at the end.

key_constructs.name: _no_trailing_dot
key_constructs.purpose: Checks whether any value element for a record field lacks a trailing '.'
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to determine whether MX/SRV fields actually need normalization before creating a modified record.

key_constructs.name: _ensure_trailing_dots
key_constructs.purpose: Appends '.' to all entries of a record field while preserving value class
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes the value transformation logic for structured record fields (MX exchange, SRV target) that are represented by value objects rather than raw strings.

key_constructs.name: EnsureTrailingDots
key_constructs.purpose: Processor that modifies desired zone records to ensure required trailing dots exist
key_constructs.reasoning: Integrates into octoDNS processor pipeline via `process_source_zone`, applying normalization during zone comparison/planning.

key_constructs.name: process_source_zone
key_constructs.purpose: Iterates desired.records and conditionally rewrites record fields that lack trailing dots
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the processor entry point used by the rest of the system; it controls when to add replacement records and how leniency is propagated.

key_behaviors: Appends a trailing '.' to ALIAS/CNAME/DNAME record.value when missing
Appends trailing '.' to each element of NS/PTR record.values when any are missing
Appends trailing '.' to MX.exchange and SRV.target fields for all relevant elements when missing
Preserves the original value subclass type by reconstructing via __class__(f'{...}.')
Replaces transformed records in the desired zone via desired.add_record(..., replace=True, lenient=lenient)

pitfalls.mistake: Treating record value fields as plain strings and setting them to f'{val}.' directly
pitfalls.consequence: Value subclass types may be lost, which can break downstream logic/tests expecting RR-specific value objects rather than raw strings
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain the current pattern of reconstructing using the existing value object's class (e.g., val.__class__(...)) when modifying any value field.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying `desired.records` without using `desired.add_record(..., replace=True, ...)`
pitfalls.consequence: The zone might contain both original and modified records or fail to reflect the intended transformation in later planning steps
pitfalls.prevention: Use `desired.add_record` with `replace=True` as done here to ensure deterministic replacement behavior.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming all record types store domain-name data in the same attribute (`record.value` vs `record.values` vs structured fields)
pitfalls.consequence: Normalization may target the wrong field, causing missing trailing dots or incorrect transformations
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the per-type attribute handling used here: single `record.value` for ALIAS/CNAME/DNAME, list `record.values` for NS/PTR, and structured property names `exchange`/`target` for MX/SRV.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the conditions that decide when to normalize (e.g., using OR/AND incorrectly across values)
pitfalls.consequence: Records could be unnecessarily rewritten (or not rewritten when needed), impacting the diff/plan output and lenient handling
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the existing conditional checks that only rewrite when at least one relevant field element lacks a trailing dot.

key_sections: process_source_zone: per-record-type branching and replace/add behavior
_ensure_trailing_dots: shared logic for structured value fields (iterating new.values and appending '.' with class preservation)
_no_trailing_dot: gating function to avoid rewriting when already normalized
skip_unless_needed: Module-level imports (only BaseProcessor is required)
Commentaries about strings vs value classes (they indicate an intentional type-preservation constraint)

relevant_snippets.description: Test suite name indicates coverage of ALIAS/DNAME/PTR/CNAME trailing-dot normalization and likely verifies correct value object types.

related_files.relationship: Defines BaseProcessor interface/lenient behavior consumed by EnsureTrailingDots
related_files.reason_to_check: If `lenient` semantics or processor method signatures change, this processor’s `process_source_zone` behavior must remain consistent.

related_files.relationship: Defines Zone and how `desired.add_record(..., replace=True, lenient=...)` behaves
related_files.reason_to_check: Replacement/deduplication semantics affect how these modified records appear in the zone planning diff.

related_files.relationship: Other processors may transform records and interact via ordering/composition
related_files.reason_to_check: If processors are chained, trailing-dot normalization relies on record structure/value classes remaining compatible.

insights.title: EnsureTrailingDots preserves RR value types when appending trailing dots
insights.problem: EnsureTrailingDots previously reverted values back to plain strings after modifications. Downstream code expected specific RR value classes (e.g., CnameValue, AliasValue, PtrValue) and broke when it received raw strings.
insights.root_cause: Implementation updated record values via string concatenation without reconstructing the original value subclass, losing type information.
insights.solution: When appending dots, construct a new instance using `val.__class__(f'{val}.')` (for single-value ALIAS/CNAME/DNAME) and capture `klass = new.values[0].__class__` then rebuild each value element as `klass(f'{v}.')` for multi-value record types (NS/PTR).
insights.lesson_learned: In a codebase with typed value objects (RR-specific value classes), always preserve the value class when transforming the underlying text; tests should assert both value equality and instance type.

octodns/processor/spf.py.short_description: Validates SPF TXT records by checking DNS lookup limits and deprecated mechanisms.
octodns/processor/spf.py.description:
This module defines an octoDNS processor (`SpfDnsLookupProcessor`) intended to validate the contents of SPF records found in DNS TXT records during zone processing. It is part of the octoDNS pipeline where processors run against records in a source zone before generating a plan or applying changes to targets. Its primary responsibility is to inspect TXT record values, extract any SPF strings (those starting with `v=spf1 `), and then analyze the SPF mechanisms/modifiers to estimate DNS lookups and reject disallowed patterns.

The processor enforces two main classes of checks: (1) it rejects TXT records containing multiple SPF strings (treated as a `permerror` per RFC references in comments), and (2) it rejects SPF mechanisms that either exceed the SPF 10 DNS lookup limit or include the deprecated `ptr` mechanism. It also recursively resolves domains for `include:` mechanisms via `dns.resolver.resolve(domain, 'TXT')`, processes the TXT answers to find nested SPF values, and then continues lookup counting through the included SPF content.

Integration-wise, `process_source_zone(self, zone, sources, lenient=False)` iterates `zone.records`, filters to TXT records only, and checks record-level leniency via `if record.lenient: continue`. Notably, the `lenient` parameter passed to `process_source_zone` is not used; behavior is driven by `record.lenient`. Exceptions are raised using module-local `ProcessorException` subclasses (`SpfValueException`, `SpfDnsLookupException`), which upstream code is expected to interpret as validation failures within the processor framework.

key_constructs.name: SpfDnsLookupProcessor
key_constructs.purpose: Processor that validates SPF TXT records against lookup limits and certain invalid/deprecated mechanisms.
key_constructs.reasoning: Encapsulates SPF-specific parsing and recursive DNS lookup counting in a processor class that the octoDNS pipeline can instantiate/configure.

key_constructs.name: process_source_zone
key_constructs.purpose: Entry point called by the octoDNS processing pipeline to validate records in a source zone.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is how the processor is applied during zone processing: it selects relevant TXT records and triggers SPF validation for each.

key_constructs.name: _get_spf_from_txt_values
key_constructs.purpose: Extracts a single SPF string from a list of TXT values, enforcing that at most one SPF string exists.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes SPF detection logic (prefix `v=spf1 `) and provides the specific error case when multiple SPF values occur.

key_constructs.name: _process_answer
key_constructs.purpose: Converts a DNS resolver `Answer` into a list of processed TXT strings suitable for SPF parsing.
key_constructs.reasoning: Bridges dnspython's `Answer` objects to the string list format expected by `_check_dns_lookups`.

key_constructs.name: _check_dns_lookups
key_constructs.purpose: Recursively analyzes SPF mechanisms/modifiers to count DNS lookups and enforce SPF-specific constraints.
key_constructs.reasoning: Implements the key validation logic: counts lookups for certain mechanisms and recursively resolves `include:` terms.

key_constructs.name: SpfValueException
key_constructs.purpose: Signals SPF content/value validation failures.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used for cases like multiple SPF values in one TXT record or deprecated `ptr` usage.

key_constructs.name: SpfDnsLookupException
key_constructs.purpose: Signals SPF validation failures specifically related to DNS lookup limit violations.
key_constructs.reasoning: Distinguishes lookup-limit failures from other SPF parsing/value errors for clearer upstream handling.

key_behaviors: Scans TXT record values and extracts SPF strings starting with the prefix `v=spf1 `
Raises an exception if multiple SPF strings are found in a single TXT record
Rejects SPF records using the deprecated `ptr` mechanism
Counts DNS lookup cost for mechanisms/modifiers matching `a`, `mx`, `exists:`, `redirect`, and `include:`
Recursively resolves and validates SPF content referenced by `include:` using dnspython TXT lookups
Stops/raises when accumulated DNS lookup count exceeds 10

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming `process_source_zone(..., lenient=...)` controls skipping validation
pitfalls.consequence: Validation will still occur unless `record.lenient` is set; changing the processor invocation may not have the intended effect.
pitfalls.prevention: When modifying behavior around leniency, confirm whether upstream sets `record.lenient` and how `lenient` argument is used elsewhere.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying parsing of SPF terms without accounting for the simplistic `split(' ')` logic
pitfalls.consequence: Lookup counting and recursion may become incorrect (e.g., terms may be misidentified), causing false negatives/positives.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the expected tokenization assumptions unless the parsing strategy is coordinated with the rest of octoDNS SPF expectations.

pitfalls.mistake: Treating dnspython `Answer` TXT formatting differently without matching `_process_answer` string cleanup
pitfalls.consequence: The SPF prefix detection (`value.startswith('v=spf1 ')`) may fail if extracted strings include unexpected quoting/concatenation artifacts.
pitfalls.prevention: When changing DNS answer processing, verify the resulting string format still matches the prefix logic.

pitfalls.mistake: Expecting non-`include:` mechanisms to trigger recursive DNS resolution
pitfalls.consequence: Only `include:` triggers recursive lookups; mechanisms like `redirect` are counted as a lookup but not resolved, which affects validation results.
pitfalls.prevention: Avoid changing lookup/resolution semantics without understanding the current counting model.

pitfalls.mistake: Allowing DNS resolution exceptions to escape without being mapped to `ProcessorException`
pitfalls.consequence: A dnspython resolution error may fail the processor in an unexpected way (e.g., crash) rather than raising `SpfDnsLookupException`/`SpfValueException`.
pitfalls.prevention: If dnspython behavior is modified or error handling added, ensure failures integrate consistently with the processor framework's expectations.

key_sections: process_source_zone: how records are filtered (TXT only) and which leniency flag actually matters (record.lenient)
_get_spf_from_txt_values: SPF string extraction and the multiple-SPF error behavior
_check_dns_lookups: main recursive lookup counting logic, including the 10-lookup enforcement and `include:` recursion
skip_unless_needed: Module-level exception class definitions (unless adjusting exception types/handling contract)

related_files.relationship: Defines `BaseProcessor` and `ProcessorException` behavior that this module subclasses and relies on for error handling and pipeline integration.
related_files.reason_to_check: If validation exceptions are handled or displayed differently, it will affect how these SPF exceptions surface to users.

related_files.relationship: `Record` API is used here for `record.fqdn`, `record._type`, `record.values`, and `record.lenient`.
related_files.reason_to_check: If record attributes or types change (e.g., `_type` vs `type`), this processor’s filtering and value access could break.

octodns/processor/meta.py.short_description: Processor that injects a meta TXT record containing time/uuid/version/provider/extra.
octodns/processor/meta.py.description:
This module defines `MetaProcessor`, a `BaseProcessor` implementation that adds a special TXT record (default name `meta`) to the desired zone during processing. The record’s TXT values are built from configured flags: optional UTC timestamp (`time=<iso>`), optional UUID (`uuid=<...>`), optional octoDNS version (`octodns-version=<...>`), optional target/provider identifier (`provider=<target_id>`), and optional arbitrary extra key/value pairs. The intent is operational observability: monitoring whether changes propagate, and identifying which backend/authority responded in dual-horizon setups.

A key behavioral constraint is that the meta record should not continually cause updates. `MetaProcessor` checks whether an existing meta TXT record is already “up to date” and suppresses the processor effect when the only change in the plan is effectively a no-op meta update. Up-to-date detection is identity-sensitive: it validates the record type is TXT, the record name matches `record_name`, and then compares only the set of value keys (the left side of `key=<value>`), not the values themselves. This allows time/uuid values to vary while still treating the meta record as unchanged if the configured key set is the same (e.g., only the value differs).

It fits into the larger octoDNS pipeline via the processor interface: `process_source_and_target_zones` mutates the `desired` zone by adding the meta record, and `process_plan` optionally returns `None` to indicate the plan should not proceed (used when the plan contains only an up-to-date meta change). The processor relies on `Record.new()` and `Record.values` semantics from `octodns.record`, and it uses the plan’s `changes` list (containing `Create`/`Update` objects) to decide whether to suppress updates.

key_constructs.name: _keys
key_constructs.purpose: Extracts the set of value keys from strings formatted like 'key=<value>'.
key_constructs.reasoning: The up-to-date check is designed to ignore the actual values for meta TXT entries and only compare which keys are present.

key_constructs.name: MetaProcessor
key_constructs.purpose: Adds/configures meta TXT record values and suppresses no-op meta-only plans.
key_constructs.reasoning: This class is the unit responsible for generating the meta record per target and deciding when meta should (or should not) force plan updates.

key_constructs.name: get_time
key_constructs.purpose: Returns current UTC time as an ISO 8601 string.
key_constructs.reasoning: Time is rendered per processor instance at initialization when `include_time` is true.

key_constructs.name: get_uuid
key_constructs.purpose: Returns a new UUID string.
key_constructs.reasoning: UUID is generated per processor instance at initialization when `include_uuid` is true.

key_constructs.name: values
key_constructs.purpose: Builds the list of TXT 'key=value' strings for the target.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is the canonical renderer used both when creating the meta record and when deciding whether an existing meta record is up to date.

key_constructs.name: process_source_and_target_zones
key_constructs.purpose: Creates and adds the meta TXT record to the desired zone.
key_constructs.reasoning: This is where the meta record is injected into the zone graph prior to planning.

key_constructs.name: _is_up_to_date_meta
key_constructs.purpose: Determines if a plan change represents a meta TXT record already matching configured keys/identity.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used to suppress plans when the only change is an effectively no-op update to the meta record.

key_constructs.name: process_plan
key_constructs.purpose: Suppresses the plan if it contains only a single meta update that is already up to date.
key_constructs.reasoning: Implements the “meta should update only when meaningful changes occur” behavior.

key_behaviors: renders meta TXT values based on include_* flags
adds a meta TXT record into the desired zone via process_source_and_target_zones
suppresses meta-only no-op plans by returning None from process_plan
compares only value keys (left side) for up-to-date meta detection

pitfalls.mistake: Treating value equality as the up-to-date criterion instead of key-set equality
pitfalls.consequence: Plans may be suppressed or triggered incorrectly, especially when time/uuid values change between runs.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the design of comparing `_keys(self.values(target_id))` vs `_keys(existing.values)` rather than full list equality.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying the identity gating (type/name) used in `_is_up_to_date_meta`
pitfalls.consequence: The processor may suppress updates for unrelated TXT records or fail to suppress its own meta no-ops, leading to unexpected plan churn.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure `_type == 'TXT'`, `record.name == self.record_name`, and `existing is not None` remain part of the logic.

pitfalls.mistake: Assuming ordering of TXT values is irrelevant without checking expectations
pitfalls.consequence: Downstream record comparison or tests may fail if list order matters (even if keys comparison is order-insensitive).
pitfalls.prevention: When changing value construction, keep in mind that tests assert exact list ordering for `values()`.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing when time/uuid are generated (constructor-time vs render-time)
pitfalls.consequence: Time/uuid may vary per call instead of per processor instance, affecting repeatability and plan suppression behavior.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect that `self.time` and `self.uuid` are set in `__init__` when the include flags are true.

key_sections: MetaProcessor.values: canonical construction of TXT key=value strings
MetaProcessor.process_source_and_target_zones: creation/injection of the meta record into desired zone
MetaProcessor._is_up_to_date_meta: identity + key-set matching used to suppress meta-only updates
MetaProcessor.process_plan: suppression rule requiring a single meta update change
skip_unless_needed: _keys helper (small, only used by up-to-date comparison)
UTC fallback import (only affects datetime tz handling)

relevant_snippets.description: Defines dummy target and base zone/records used across tests.
Asserts MetaProcessor.values() output for various include_* configurations and ordering.
Validates _is_up_to_date_meta behavior: suppression only for TXT meta with matching name/type.
Ensures process_source_and_target_zones injects correct meta record values per flags.
Checks process_plan suppression semantics (meta-only and up-to-date) vs preserving other changes.

related_files.relationship: Unit tests that codify value ordering, identity-sensitive up-to-date rules, and plan suppression conditions.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any changes to value rendering or suppression rules must keep these assertions passing.

insights.title: Simplify MetaProcessor by separating config time/uuid/provider/version from per-target rendering
insights.problem: MetaProcessor logic intertwined time/uuid/provider injection with record scanning/updating, and contained up-to-date detection that depended on full value equality rather than key equivalence.
insights.root_cause: MetaProcessor mixed concerns: generating meta values, applying provider-specific fields, and determining plan safety. This made it more complex to reason about and more brittle for up-to-date checks.
insights.solution: Refactored to store time/uuid/config toggles, added values(target_id) to produce the exact meta value list for a specific target, simplified process_source_and_target_zones to create meta records with correct values directly, and adjusted _is_up_to_date_meta to compare keys/fields rather than full value sets.
insights.lesson_learned: For dynamic per-target meta content, compute values lazily per target and keep up-to-date checks focused on meaningful fields/keys; simplify flow to reduce brittle branching.

insights.title: MetaProcessor _up_to_date check could fail for single-change plans (e.g., CNAME meta value scenario)
insights.problem: The “up-to-date” detection for meta changes was too broad and could incorrectly conclude a plan’s meta record needed no changes, or fail to detect it correctly when plans had a single change type/value scenario.
insights.root_cause: The previous logic in MetaProcessor._up_to_date only compared existing.values keys vs self.values keys, without ensuring the change being evaluated was actually the expected meta TXT record (type and name). That made the check sensitive to unrelated TXT records or mismatched record identity.
insights.solution:
Refactored the check into _is_up_to_date_meta(change) and tightened conditions to require:
- change.record._type == 'TXT'
- change.record.name == self.record_name
- existing is not None
- _keys(existing.values) matches _keys(self.values)
Then updated the plan logic to use this stricter check.
insights.lesson_learned: When implementing “up-to-date” logic in processors, always validate record identity (type/name) in addition to value equality—especially for meta/special-purpose records that may coexist with other TXT records.

tests/test_octodns_processor_meta.py.short_description: Unit tests for MetaProcessor value rendering, up-to-date detection, and plan suppression.
tests/test_octodns_processor_meta.py.description:
This file provides focused unit coverage for `MetaProcessor`. It sets up a lightweight `Zone('unit.tests.', [])` and constructs representative `Record.new` instances used to simulate meta vs non-meta records and TXT vs non-TXT records. A `DummyTarget` provides a stable `id` used when rendering provider-specific meta values.

The tests establish contractual expectations around three main behaviors: (1) `MetaProcessor.values(target_id)` renders specific `key=value` strings with exact ordering for each include flag combination, (2) `_is_up_to_date_meta(change, target_id)` uses identity-sensitive checks (TXT type and exact name match) and only compares value keys (not actual time/uuid values), and (3) `process_source_and_target_zones` injects a meta TXT record into the desired zone without mutating the existing zone. Finally, `process_plan` is exercised to ensure meta-only no-op updates are suppressed (returns `None`/falsey), while other changes in the plan are preserved even if the meta record is up to date.

Time and UUID are made deterministic using `@patch` on `MetaProcessor.get_time` and `MetaProcessor.get_uuid` in the tests, which implies the processor generates time/uuid at construction time rather than at render time. Any code edits that change this lifecycle would require test expectations to align with the new behavior.

key_constructs.name: TestMetaProcessor
key_constructs.purpose: Test suite validating MetaProcessor behavior across configuration, injection, and plan suppression.
key_constructs.reasoning: Centralizes fixtures and multiple test scenarios in a consistent zone/record setup.

key_constructs.name: test_args_and_values
key_constructs.purpose: Verifies MetaProcessor.values output for default and various include flag combinations.
key_constructs.reasoning: The tests assert exact list contents and ordering, effectively defining rendering order as part of the contract.

key_constructs.name: test_is_up_to_date_meta
key_constructs.purpose: Ensures _is_up_to_date_meta suppresses only the correct meta TXT update identity.
key_constructs.reasoning: Covers negative cases: non-meta name and non-TXT record types, and ensures Create changes are never treated as up to date.

key_constructs.name: test_process_plan
key_constructs.purpose: Validates process_plan suppression rules for meta-only plans vs plans with additional changes.
key_constructs.reasoning: Locks in the behavior that only a single, up-to-date meta update may be suppressed.

key_behaviors: patches MetaProcessor.get_time/get_uuid to make outputs deterministic
asserts exact ordering of rendered TXT values
asserts identity-sensitive up-to-date logic for meta TXT records
verifies plan suppression only when meta is the only change

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the rendering order of values() without updating tests
pitfalls.consequence: Tests will fail due to exact list equality expectations (not just set membership).
pitfalls.prevention: Keep ordering expectations in mind when modifying value construction.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the time/uuid generation lifecycle (constructor-time vs call-time)
pitfalls.consequence: Patched mocks in tests may no longer intercept the correct calls, causing nondeterministic assertions.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the assumption that __init__ calls get_time/get_uuid when include flags are enabled.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering identity criteria in _is_up_to_date_meta
pitfalls.consequence: Negative test cases (wrong name/type) would no longer be correctly filtered, causing tests to fail.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain record identity checks in the up-to-date logic used by these tests.

key_sections: Fixtures: meta_needs_update/meta_up_to_date/not_meta/not_txt
test_args_and_values: contract for ordering and key/value string formats
test_is_up_to_date_meta: identity checks and Create vs Update semantics
test_process_source_and_target_zones: injection behavior into desired zone
test_process_plan: suppression criteria across plan composition
skip_unless_needed: test_uuid and test_extra are shorter contract tests for specific config modes

related_files.relationship: Implements the MetaProcessor behavior under test; tests mirror its value ordering and suppression semantics.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any behavioral change in processor logic must be consistent with these expectations.

insights.title: Added explicit unit coverage for MetaProcessor up-to-date detection identity (TXT type/name)
insights.problem: Existing tests did not cover cases where a change is a TXT but not the expected meta TXT record, or where identity checks were missing.
insights.root_cause: The previous test coverage likely validated only value-matching behavior, not that the processor ensured the change being checked corresponded to its own meta record (type/name).
insights.solution:
Updated/renamed test_up_to_date to test_is_up_to_date_meta and added negative cases:
- TXT meta record mismatch because it’s not the meta record type/name (uses a CNAME record for type mismatch)
- TXT record type mismatch / non-meta TXT record scenario
This ensures the new identity gating behavior is enforced.
insights.lesson_learned: For processor “is up-to-date” behavior, include negative tests for record identity mismatches (type and/or name), not just value mismatches.

octodns/processor/ownership.py.short_description: Processor that injects TXT ownership records and filters plan changes to only owned records.
octodns/processor/ownership.py.description:
This module defines `OwnershipProcessor`, a `BaseProcessor` implementation that makes octoDNS manageability explicit by creating special TXT records (default `_owner.*octodns*`) alongside records it is configured to manage. The generated TXT “ownership” records are used later to decide whether a plan change targeting an existing record is allowed: if the processor can determine a record is owned (via the presence of the appropriate ownership TXT), then it will keep and apply changes; otherwise it will ignore them.

`process_source_zone` runs during configuration/zone preprocessing. For each desired record in the source zone, it synthesizes a TXT ownership record whose name encodes the record type and the record name, including special handling for wildcard record names (replacing `*` with `_wildcard`). It intentionally skips creating ownership records for records that already look like ownership TXTs to avoid double-processing issues, especially in alias zones that might be processed more than once.

`process_plan` runs after planning. It builds an `owned` index by scanning both `plan.existing.records` and `plan.desired.records` for ownership TXTs, then filters `plan.changes` based on ownership status. It always ignores changes for records that are neither ownership TXTs nor owned by the processor’s TXT markers, except for the special record name `octodns-meta` which is always left alone. If filtering results in no changes, it returns `None`; if filtering changes the list, it constructs a new `Plan` with the filtered changes, preserving the other threshold fields from the original plan.

key_constructs.name: OwnershipProcessor
key_constructs.purpose: Synthesizes TXT ownership markers for desired records and filters plan changes based on ownership.
key_constructs.reasoning: This processor is designed to be idempotent across runs and safe with alias-zone double-processing by (a) skipping existing ownership TXT records in source processing and (b) optionally allowing replacement when adding duplicate ownership TXTs.

key_constructs.name: process_source_zone
key_constructs.purpose: Creates ownership TXT records for non-ownership desired records in a zone.
key_constructs.reasoning: It centralizes the naming convention for ownership TXT records and ensures every managed record has a corresponding ownership marker, optionally using the `replace` strategy controlled by `should_replace`.

key_constructs.name: _is_ownership
key_constructs.purpose: Determines whether a record matches the processor’s ownership TXT signature (type/name/value).
key_constructs.reasoning: Used both during source processing (to avoid re-adding ownership) and during plan processing (to index owned records).

key_constructs.name: process_plan
key_constructs.purpose: Filters a computed plan to keep only ownership-relevant changes (owned records and ownership TXTs).
key_constructs.reasoning: Implements the ownership-based safety mechanism: changes to existing records not owned by octoDNS are ignored, preventing accidental deletion or modification.

key_behaviors: Creates ownership TXT records for each desired non-ownership record in `process_source_zone`
Skips adding ownership TXT when the desired record already matches ownership signature via `_is_ownership`
Encodes ownership TXT names using record type and record name, with wildcard `*` mapped to `_wildcard`
Scans plan existing+desired records to infer ownership coverage in `process_plan`
Filters plan changes to only include (a) ownership TXTs, (b) records whose names appear as owned, and (c) records not filtered when `octodns-meta`
Returns `None` when filtering would remove all changes from the plan

pitfalls.mistake: Treating `ownership TXT` records the same as normal desired records (i.e., not skipping them).
pitfalls.consequence: Ownership TXT creation can be attempted multiple times (common in alias-zone scenarios), leading to duplicate record failures during zone processing.
pitfalls.prevention: Respect the `_is_ownership` check in `process_source_zone` so processor-produced ownership records are not reprocessed.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering the ownership TXT naming convention without updating plan parsing logic.
pitfalls.consequence: The `process_plan` owned-index parsing (`name.split('.', 2)` and `_wildcard` → `*`) may stop matching, causing unintended filtering (either over-permissive changes or no changes at all).
pitfalls.prevention: When changing naming, ensure both source creation and plan ownership indexing remain consistent.

pitfalls.mistake: Removing `lenient=True` from ownership TXT insertion.
pitfalls.consequence: Plans/processing may fail in special DNS delegation/zone-subzone layouts where the ownership record is not located where the core record lives.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the lenient add semantics as used by existing tests and expected behavior.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing the semantics of what is considered special/unowned (e.g., removing `record.name != 'octodns-meta'` guard).
pitfalls.consequence: The processor could start deleting or ignoring changes for meta records that are expected to be managed differently.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the `octodns-meta` exception behavior intact.

key_sections: OwnershipProcessor.__init__: establishes ownership TXT parameters and `should_replace` behavior
OwnershipProcessor.process_source_zone: ownership TXT naming + Record.new + desired.add_record(lenient=True, replace=should_replace)
OwnershipProcessor._is_ownership: exact match on TXT type, name prefix, and values list equality
OwnershipProcessor.process_plan: builds `owned` map from both existing+desired, then filters plan.changes and returns None or new Plan
skip_unless_needed: The top module comments; no additional logic beyond the class.

relevant_snippets.description: Defines a unit Zone with several record types (A/AAAA/TXT and wildcard record) used by the processor tests.
Asserts `process_source_zone` produces the expected ownership record names and verifies default TXT TTL (60) and `_is_ownership` detection.
Asserts `process_plan` is a noop when plan is None, does not remove ownership-covered changes, and ignores changes for unowned extra records.
Asserts owned-record deletes are allowed through `process_plan` by constructing ownership TXT + delete changes.
Asserts `process_plan` returns None when the only change(s) are filtered away (remove last change).
Asserts duplicate ownership TXT additions raise DuplicateRecordException by default, but succeed when `should_replace=True`.

related_files.relationship: Unit tests validate ownership naming, TTL config, duplicate handling, and plan-change filtering behavior.
related_files.reason_to_check: Any change to ownership record naming, ownership matching, or plan filtering will likely require aligning these assertions.

insights.title: Add should_replace to OwnershipProcessor to handle alias-zone double-processing
insights.problem: When processing alias zones, ownership TXT records could be added twice (once for the original and again for the alias), causing duplicate record exceptions and preventing plans from completing.
insights.root_cause: OwnershipProcessor did not have a configurable replacement strategy when adding ownership TXT records in cases where alias processing leads to previously-added ownership records being re-attempted.
insights.solution: Introduced `should_replace` parameter stored on the processor, passed into `desired.add_record(txt, lenient=True, replace=self.should_replace)`. Also preserved logic to skip applying ownership to records that already look like ownership records (`_is_ownership`). Added tests verifying default failure on duplicates and success when `should_replace=True`.
insights.lesson_learned: For processors that synthesize records, provide an explicit `replace`/idempotency mechanism for scenarios involving multiple passes (e.g., alias zones).

insights.title: Make OwnershipProcessor TXT TTL configurable (default preserved)
insights.problem: OwnershipProcessor always emitted the TXT record with a hard-coded TTL (60), making it impossible to align with environments/providers requiring a different TTL.
insights.root_cause: TXT TTL was not exposed as a processor parameter; TTL was embedded in the record construction.
insights.solution: Added `txt_ttl` parameter to `OwnershipProcessor.__init__` (default `60`) and used `self.txt_ttl` when building the TXT record in `_process`.
insights.lesson_learned: When processor output affects record semantics (like TTL), expose it as an explicit config knob and keep a backwards-compatible default.

tests/test_octodns_processor_ownership.p.short_description: Tests OwnershipProcessor source-zone ownership TXT generation and plan filtering semantics.
tests/test_octodns_processor_ownership.p.description:
This unit test module exercises `OwnershipProcessor` end-to-end at two integration points: generating ownership TXT records during `process_source_zone`, and filtering plan changes during `process_plan`. It constructs an in-memory `Zone` with multiple record types, including a wildcard-record case (`Record.new(zone, '*', ...)`) to validate how the processor encodes wildcard names into ownership TXT names.

The test suite also covers critical safety behaviors: (1) `process_plan(None, ...)` must be a quick noop, (2) when a plan introduces only records the processor is set up to manage (ownership markers included), `process_plan` should preserve all changes, and (3) if a plan proposes changes to an extra existing record that lacks ownership markers, `process_plan` should drop those changes. A dedicated test validates that when filtering removes the last remaining change, `process_plan` returns a falsey value (None/empty result) rather than a plan with zero changes.

Finally, the tests validate the duplicate-handling behavior governed by `should_replace`. By default, adding ownership records to a zone that already contains the ownership TXT should fail with `DuplicateRecordException`. When `should_replace=True`, the processor should be able to re-add ownership TXT records without raising, ensuring idempotency across repeated processing passes.

key_constructs.name: TestOwnershipProcessor
key_constructs.purpose: Group of unit tests validating OwnershipProcessor behavior.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides scenario-based coverage for ownership TXT generation, TTL configuration, plan filtering, and replacement semantics.

key_constructs.name: test_process_source_zone
key_constructs.purpose: Asserts generated ownership TXT names, values, and default/override TTL behavior.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures the ownership naming convention matches what `process_plan` expects and that TTL configuration is honored.

key_constructs.name: test_process_plan

tests/test_octodns_yaml.py.short_description: Unit tests for octodns.yaml safe_load/safe_dump: ordering, include, and merge behavior
tests/test_octodns_yaml.py.description:
This file is a focused test suite validating the expected YAML parsing and serialization semantics implemented in `octodns.yaml` (imported as `InvalidOrder`, `safe_dump`, `safe_load`). It primarily asserts deterministic handling of YAML mappings whose keys include octodns-style wildcard patterns (e.g., `*.1.2`, `*.10.1`), including strict ordering validation during load and stable ordering during dump.

The tests also validate a custom YAML feature: including other YAML files and merging included content into a parent YAML document. These behaviors are exercised via fixtures under `tests/config/include/`, and the suite asserts both correct merge results (including overwrite precedence) and correct error behavior when an include path does not exist. Finally, it checks that ordering mode can be switched (`order_mode='natural'` vs `'simple'`) and that invalid `order_mode` values raise `InvalidOrder` with an exact error message.

As a result, any edits to `octodns.yaml` must preserve the same external contract that this suite encodes: stable key ordering rules, consistent dumping output formatting (including quoting behavior), include path resolution and merge precedence, and the exact exception types/messages used for validation and configuration errors.

key_constructs.name: TestYaml
key_constructs.purpose: Container for YAML-related unit tests.
key_constructs.reasoning: Understanding this class clarifies which behaviors are considered stable public contracts (exception types/messages and output formatting).

key_constructs.name: test_stuff
key_constructs.purpose: Validates safe_load ordering validation and safe_dump deterministic ordering/formatting, including non-hex key ordering behavior.
key_constructs.reasoning: This test encodes multiple independent invariants: correct parsing, strict order errors for wildcard keys, stable dump ordering, and preserving existing behavior for keys that look like hex.

key_constructs.name: test_include
key_constructs.purpose: Validates include loading from fixture YAMLs and error propagation when included files do not exist.
key_constructs.reasoning: Ensures include resolution and error messages are stable, which can be easy to accidentally break when modifying YAML loading logic.

key_constructs.name: test_include_merge
key_constructs.purpose: Validates how included YAML merges into the parent when `enforce_order=False` is passed to safe_load.
key_constructs.reasoning: This test captures precedence rules between parent keys and include keys, including nested dict merging semantics.

key_constructs.name: test_order_mode
key_constructs.purpose: Validates ordering enforcement differences between `order_mode='natural'` and `order_mode='simple'`, plus handling of invalid order_mode values.
key_constructs.reasoning: This test is the sole source of expected ordering differences and exact error message for invalid order_mode inputs.

handles_entities: Mapping keys with octodns wildcard patterns
key_behaviors: parses YAML mapping keys into Python dicts while enforcing expected key order rules
raises ConstructorError when YAML mapping keys are out of order under strict ordering
serializes mappings to YAML with deterministic key ordering and specific quoting/formatting
loads YAML with custom include directives and merges included content into parent documents
raises FileNotFoundError with a stable message when an include target file is missing
switches key ordering enforcement behavior via order_mode parameter
raises InvalidOrder for unsupported order_mode values with an exact message

pitfalls.mistake: Changing exception types (e.g., raising a different parsing exception instead of `yaml.constructor.ConstructorError` for out-of-order keys).
pitfalls.consequence: Assertions using `with self.assertRaises(ConstructorError)` and checks of `ctx.exception.problem` would fail, breaking contract compatibility.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the same exception class and ensure the `.problem` string content remains compatible with the tested substrings.

pitfalls.mistake: Modifying dump ordering logic or formatting (quoting of wildcard keys, newline placement, or the order of keys in output).
pitfalls.consequence: Tests compare full output strings (e.g., `buf.getvalue()` including trailing newline), so any formatting drift fails assertions.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat the exact output strings in assertions as strict acceptance criteria for safe_dump behavior.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering include merge precedence (which document overwrites which keys in nested dicts).
pitfalls.consequence: The expected merged structure in `test_include_merge` would not match, indicating a behavioral regression in include semantics.
pitfalls.prevention: Maintain the established precedence rules between parent and included values as encoded by expected dict output.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing include error message wording when a file is missing.
pitfalls.consequence: The test asserts `str(ctx.exception)` equals an exact string containing the include path.
pitfalls.prevention: Ensure file path formatting and exception propagation remain consistent.

pitfalls.mistake: Changing order_mode validation behavior or error message text for invalid order_mode values.
pitfalls.consequence: The suite checks `str(ctx.exception)` exactly for both `safe_load` and `safe_dump` invalid order_mode inputs.
pitfalls.prevention: Keep the exact InvalidOrder message text and parameter validation behavior aligned with the tests.

octodns/record/__init__.py.short_description: Re-exports public record/value classes for the octodns.record package.
octodns/record/__init__.py.description:
This module exists primarily as the public API surface for the `octodns.record` package. It imports record/value classes (e.g., `ARecord`, `AaaaRecord`, `CnameRecord`, `TxtRecord`, etc.) and exception types (`RecordException`, `ValidationError`) from the underlying per-record modules, then re-exports them at the package level so callers can import from `octodns.record` rather than from each specific submodule.

The long sequence of bare symbol references at the bottom (“# quell warnings” followed by `ARecord`, `AaaaRecord`, etc.) is used to silence linting/static analysis warnings about imported-but-unused names. In practice, these symbols are expected to be accessed via `octodns.record.<Symbol>` imports; without the re-export usage, some tooling may consider the imports unused. Any edits to this file must preserve the intent of exporting the same public symbols and keeping tooling quiet.

Within the larger system, record classes are core domain objects used when parsing configuration, representing DNS records, and generating provider plans/applies. While this `__init__.py` does not implement record logic itself, it directly affects import paths used throughout the codebase (and potentially by external users). Changing, removing, or renaming an export here can break imports even if underlying submodules still work.

key_constructs.reasoning: Callers can use `from octodns.record import ARecord`; removing or changing this export would break those imports.

key_constructs.purpose: Imported/exported exception raised for record-related failures.
key_constructs.reasoning: Used as a stable error type across the project; the package-level export enables consistent exception handling.

key_constructs.name: ValidationError
key_constructs.purpose: Imported/exported validation error type for invalid record definitions.
key_constructs.reasoning: If callers catch `ValidationError` from the package root, this export must remain intact.

key_constructs.name: Record
key_constructs.purpose: Imported/exported base record class and shared interfaces.
key_constructs.reasoning: Establishes the common type for all record implementations; many parts of the system likely rely on it.

key_constructs.name: ValueMixin
key_constructs.purpose: Imported/exported mixin for record value representations.
key_constructs.reasoning: Provides a shared base for value parsing/formatting behaviors; should remain available at the same import path.

silences linter warnings by referencing imported symbols

pitfalls.consequence: ImportErrors at runtime/test time even if the underlying submodule exists.
pitfalls.prevention: Treat this file as the stable public import surface; ensure any changes keep the same set of exported names.

pitfalls.mistake: Altering the “quell warnings” bare-symbol section without understanding why it exists.
pitfalls.consequence: Linting/static-analysis failures due to unused imports, or inconsistent CI behavior depending on tooling.
pitfalls.prevention: Preserve the pattern that marks exports as used if the repo’s CI/linting expects it.

pitfalls.mistake: Introducing name collisions or omitting record/value pairs (e.g., exporting `ARecord` but not `Ipv4Address`/`Ipv4Value`).
pitfalls.consequence: Downstream code that relies on specific value class exports will fail to import or behave unexpectedly.
pitfalls.prevention: Verify the exports list matches what callers expect for both record and value classes.
